And that's the problem that I see with all this DOGE stuff, because everybody should be celebrating that we've found a way to cut out fraud and waste. Yeah, if you pay taxes and you don't like that you have to pay so much in taxes, and then you find out that there's significant fraud and waste that's been exposed, you should be celebrating it. This shouldn't be 'oh no, the wrong people found this fact and now it's a bad thing.' Yes.
turns.zh: 所以我的意思是，基本上，嗯，你知道，民主党领导层或，你知道，政治领导层，他们下达了指示，他们的傀儡执行了。是啊，他们就像木偶戏里的木偶。这就是我在所有这些 DOGE 事情中看到的问题，因为每个人都应该庆祝我们找到了削减欺诈和浪费的方法。是啊，如果你纳税，你不喜欢交那么多税，然后你发现存在大量欺诈和浪费被曝光，你应该庆祝。这不应该是‘哦不，错误的人发现了这个事实，现在它成了坏事。’是的。

turns.en: And then there's the propaganda, the mind of calling it 'USAID' instead of 'the United States Agency for International Development.' It sounds like it's feeding hungry people. Where people are going to starve, Elon, this is horrible. And then you find out actually it's like $250 million for transgender animal studies, literally mutilating animals. Yes, mutilating animals in demented studies. Yes, uh, that are like the worst thing you could possibly imagine from a horror show. The beagle one, the beagle puppy one, where they covered their head in a basket and put fleas on their head so they eat them alive. Yeah, and then they studied these beagles and then killed them. Like, what are you going to learn from that? That's good for anybody? Yeah, this is some really psychotic stuff that happens.
turns.zh: 然后还有宣传，故意叫它‘USAID’而不是‘美国国际开发署’。听起来像是在给饥饿的人提供食物。人们要挨饿了，埃隆，这太可怕了。然后你发现实际上有 2.5 亿美元用于跨性别动物研究， literally 残害动物。是的，在疯狂的研究中残害动物。是的，呃，就像你能从恐怖秀中想象到的最糟糕的事情。那个比格犬实验，比格犬幼崽实验，他们把它们的头罩在篮子里，把跳蚤放在它们头上，让跳蚤活活吃掉它们。是啊，然后他们研究这些比格犬，然后杀了它们。比如，你能从中学到什么？这对任何人都有好处吗？是啊，这真是些精神病般的事情。

turns.en: So, um, yeah. I mean, the, I guess the, uh, the real threat here is to the bureaucracy. So, um, like you probably saw, like, you know, let's say like 'Trump is a threat to our democracy,' which is ironic since he was elected with a majority of the, you know, popular vote. Um, they started saying I was a threat to democracy. But if you just replace 'threat to democracy' with 'threat to bureaucracy,' it makes total sense. Right? So, um, I mean the reality is that our elected officials have very, very little power relative to the bureaucracy, until DOGE. So DOGE is a threat to the bureaucracy. It's the first threat to the bureaucracy. Normally the bureaucracy eats revolutions for breakfast. This is the first time that...
turns.zh: 所以，嗯，是啊。我的意思是，我猜，呃，真正的威胁是对官僚机构。所以，嗯，就像你可能看到的，比如，你知道，比如说‘特朗普对我们的民主构成威胁’，这很讽刺，因为他赢得了多数普选票。嗯，他们开始说我对民主构成威胁。但如果你把‘对民主的威胁’换成‘对官僚机构的威胁’，那就完全说得通了。对吧？所以，嗯，我的意思是，现实是，我们的民选官员相对于官僚机构权力非常非常小，直到 DOGE 出现。所以 DOGE 是对官僚机构的威胁。这是对官僚机构的第一次威胁。通常官僚机构把革命当早餐吃。这是第一次……

ts.sec: The Scale of Government Waste and NGO Fraud

turns.en: When you guys first started investigating it, how much of it was shocking, like the size of it all?
turns.zh: 你们刚开始调查时，规模之大有多令人震惊？

turns.en: Well, the size of it all—the small decisions result in multi-billion dollar outcomes. We saw a case where one person was getting $1.9 billion sent to their NGO, which basically got formed about a year ago and had no prior activity. So they just stand up an NGO. The whole NGO thing is a nightmare. It's a misnomer because if you have a government-funded non-governmental organization, you're simply a government-funded organization. It's an oxymoron, right? It's a loophole. Basically, government-funded NGOs are a way to do things that would be illegal if they were the government, but are somehow made legal if sent to a so-called nonprofit. But these nonprofits are then used to cash out. People become very wealthy through these nonprofits; they pay themselves enormous sums. It's so insane that it's been going on for so long. It's a gigantic scam, one of the biggest, maybe the biggest scam ever.
turns.zh: 规模之大——小决策导致数十亿美元的结果。我们见过一个案例，有人获得了19亿美元拨给他们的非政府组织，而这个组织大约一年前才成立，之前没有任何活动。所以他们就是成立了一个非政府组织。整个非政府组织的事情是一场噩梦。这是个用词不当，因为如果你有一个政府资助的非政府组织，你就是一个政府资助的组织。这是个矛盾修辞，对吧？这是个漏洞。基本上，政府资助的非政府组织是一种方式，去做如果由政府来做就会违法的事情，但通过所谓的非营利组织就合法了。但这些非营利组织被用来套现。人们通过这些非营利组织变得非常富有；他们给自己支付巨额薪酬。这太疯狂了，而且已经持续了这么久。这是一个巨大的骗局，最大的骗局之一，也许是有史以来最大的骗局。

turns.en: How many NGOs are there?
turns.zh: 有多少个非政府组织？

turns.en: I think the total number of NGOs is probably millions, but in terms of large NGOs, tens of thousands. It's a hack to the system where someone can get an NGO stood up for a fairly small amount of money. George Soros was really good at this. He figured out how to hack the system; he's a genius at arbitrage. He figured out that you could leverage a small amount of money to create a nonprofit, then lobby politicians to send a ton of money to that nonprofit. So you can take a $10 million donation to create a nonprofit and leverage that into a billion-dollar NGO. The government continues to fund that every year, and it'll have a nice-sounding name like the Institute for Peace or something, but really it's a graft machine.
turns.zh: 我认为非政府组织的总数可能有数百万，但就大型非政府组织而言，有数万个。这是对系统的一种黑客手段，一个人可以用相当少的钱成立一个非政府组织。乔治·索罗斯非常擅长这个。他搞清楚了如何黑进系统；他是个套利天才。他发现你可以用少量资金创建一个非营利组织，然后游说政客向该非营利组织投入大量资金。所以你可以用1000万美元的捐款创建一个非营利组织，然后杠杆化成一个十亿美元的非政府组织。政府每年继续资助它，它还会有一个好听的名字，比如和平研究所之类的，但实际上它是一个贪污机器。

turns.en: What are their requirements with that money? What do they have to do?
turns.zh: 他们对这笔钱有什么要求？他们需要做什么？

turns.en: Really no requirements at all. They just get grants, and the government just assumes they're doing good work. I think a lot of people in the government know they are not doing good work, but it's a giant graft machine.
turns.zh: 真的没有任何要求。他们只是获得拨款，政府就假设他们在做好事。我认为政府里很多人知道他们没在做好事，但这是一个巨大的贪污机器。

turns.en: People online are unpacking this. It almost seems fake. We were covering an article that said 55,000 Democrat NGOs were discovered that had been contributing to campaigns, moving things around, pushing propaganda, and they were all connected. They found with AI that you have to go through steps and steps to figure out where the money's coming from. It's all funneling down to this group. It's a giant propaganda machine, a giant regime change machine.
turns.zh: 网上的人正在剖析这件事。这几乎看起来像假的。我们报道了一篇文章，说发现了55,000个民主党非政府组织，它们一直在为竞选捐款、转移资金、推动宣传，而且它们都有关联。他们用人工智能发现，你必须经过层层步骤才能弄清楚钱从哪里来。所有这些都汇集到这一个团体。这是一个巨大的宣传机器，一个巨大的政权更迭机器。

turns.en: Yes, yes. But doesn't it do some good as well? No, it does some good. It's not like 0% good. If it were 0% good, it would be much easier to attack. So they add in some percent good, but it might be 5% or 10% good, and 90-95% not.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。但它不也做一些好事吗？不，它确实做一些好事。并不是0%是好的。如果是0%是好的，那就更容易攻击了。所以他们掺入了一些好的部分，但可能只有5%或10%是好的，90-95%不是。

turns.en: Is there a way to audit all this stuff and find out: oh, these people are actually just sending food to poor people, these people are actually just helping people with water in third world countries? Is there a way to do that and keep funding those?
turns.zh: 有没有办法审计所有这些，找出：哦，这些人实际上只是在给穷人送食物，这些人实际上只是在第三世界国家帮助人们获得水？有没有办法做到这一点，并继续资助那些？

turns.en: Yeah, we have continued to fund things that appear to be legitimate, even with the flimsiest excuse. If there's even the flimsiest excuse, like just say 'send me a picture of the thing,' you could literally have AI generate the picture, but if you're not even willing to try to trick me, then we're not going to send the money.
turns.zh: 是的，我们继续资助那些看起来合法的事情，即使是最站不住脚的借口。如果连最站不住脚的借口都没有，比如就说‘给我发一张照片’，你完全可以用AI生成照片，但如果你甚至不愿意尝试骗我，那我们就不给钱。

turns.en: What restrictions were put on? Was there something set aside like medicine or Ebola prevention?
turns.zh: 有什么限制措施？有没有像药品或埃博拉预防这样的专项拨款？

turns.en: I actually don't know if this work is even effective. It may not be. It could be the kind of thing where you fund Ebola prevention, but it turns out you're funding a lab that develops new Ebola virus recipes or something. They claim it's Ebola prevention, but it's actually Ebola creation. Some of these things I don't know. But it just seems we shouldn't be sending taxpayer money to dubious enterprises overseas, right?
turns.zh: 我实际上不知道这项工作是否有效。可能没有效果。可能是那种你资助埃博拉预防，结果却资助了一个开发新埃博拉病毒配方的实验室之类的事情。他们声称是埃博拉预防，但实际上是埃博拉制造。有些事我不知道。但看起来我们不应该把纳税人的钱送到海外可疑的企业，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. And why are we doing it? Is it because we want to make friends with these people so the Chinese don't take over, the Russians don't take over? How much of that is a good thing, how much is smart to do, and how much is a grift? Without any sort of oversight, it's been going on for so long, they've just had free rein.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。我们为什么要这么做？是因为我们想和这些人交朋友，这样中国人就不会接管，俄罗斯人就不会接管？其中有多少是好事，有多少是明智之举，又有多少是骗局？没有任何监督，已经持续了这么久，他们完全不受约束。

turns.en: Well, we also have a real issue with the budget deficit. It's gigantic. If we didn't have a gigantic budget deficit, where interest payments on the national debt exceed the defense department budget—which is truly astounding, meaning we're paying over a trillion dollars of interest on the national debt—then okay, we'd have more room for wasting money. But when we're spending so much money that the country is going bankrupt, we really need to stop spending money unless we're sure it's good value. So essentially, we're like a poorly managed business with an unlimited credit line that is off the rails.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们还有一个真正的问题，就是预算赤字。它非常巨大。如果我们没有巨大的预算赤字，而国债利息支出超过了国防部预算——这确实令人震惊，意味着我们支付超过一万亿美元的国债利息——那么好吧，我们会有更多浪费钱的空间。但是当我们花钱太多，国家快要破产时，我们真的需要停止花钱，除非我们确定它物有所值。所以本质上，我们就像一家管理不善、拥有无限信用额度、已经失控的企业。

turns.en: Absolutely. And if you were a person like you who comes in and takes over businesses and straightens them out, that's exactly what you're doing. I mean, most of the time I create businesses from scratch. Twitter was a case where I kind of bought a company that I knew was a hairball.
turns.zh: 绝对如此。如果你是一个像你一样进来接管企业并整顿它们的人，那正是你在做的。我的意思是，大多数时候我是从零开始创建企业。推特是一个例子，我算是买了一个我知道是一团乱麻的公司。

turns.en: Well, you came in at Tesla in the beginning, but they were already doing something right? No, Tesla did not exist in any meaningful form. There were no employees. J.B. Straubel and I joined three other people. There was no car, no nothing. There wasn't even a prototype yet.
turns.zh: 嗯，你一开始就加入了特斯拉，但他们已经在做对的事情了吗？不，特斯拉并不以任何有意义的形式存在。没有员工。J.B. Straubel 和我加入了另外三个人。没有车，什么都没有。甚至还没有原型。

turns.en: Oh, okay. I thought there was a prototype already. No, there weren't even any employees. Oh, that's a funny narrative that people like to say that you didn't even create Tesla then. Yeah, that's wrong.
turns.zh: 哦，好吧。我以为已经有原型了。不，甚至没有任何员工。哦，这是个有趣的叙事，人们喜欢说你甚至没有创造特斯拉。是的，那是错的。

turns.en: So if you're handling the government like...
turns.zh: 所以如果你在处理政府事务就像……

ts.sec: Scaling DOGE to the Federal Government

turns.en: A business you're going to have to go through all of these departments and do the exact same thing that you're doing with USA. So how does that scale up? How many people do you need to do something like that?
turns.zh: 你要把所有这些部门都过一遍，做和在美国一样的事情。那怎么规模化？需要多少人来做？

turns.en: Well, we started off with about 40 people, maybe 100 people. And really just going through doing very basic things here. It's as bad as Twitter was, the federal government is much worse. In the case of Twitter, it wasn't a profitable company, it was basically a break-even company, but at least it was break-even and it had to pass an audit. The federal government is not break-even, it's literally losing $2 trillion a year, and it does not pass its audits, it fails its own audits. There's a case where Senator Collins was telling me about how she gave the Navy $12 billion for more submarines, got no extra submarines, and then held a hearing to say 'Where'd the $12 billion go?' and they were like 'We don't know.' That was it. The stuff is so crazy, it's like only the federal government could get away with this level of waste. It's mostly waste, mostly ridiculous things happening, because they've been able to do it this way for so long and they've become accustomed to it.
turns.zh: 我们一开始大约有40人，可能100人。真的只是做一些非常基础的事情。和Twitter一样糟糕，但联邦政府更糟。Twitter不是盈利公司，基本盈亏平衡，但至少它平衡了，而且必须通过审计。联邦政府不是盈亏平衡，它每年亏损2万亿美元，而且不通过审计，连自己的审计都过不了。有个例子：柯林斯参议员告诉我，她给了海军120亿美元造更多潜艇，结果一艘都没多，然后开听证会问‘那120亿去哪了？’他们回答‘不知道’。就这样。事情太疯狂了，只有联邦政府能逃脱这种程度的浪费。主要是浪费，主要是荒谬的事情，因为他们这样做了太久，已经习惯了。

turns.en: Yeah, it's like Milton Friedman said: money is most poorly spent when you're spending someone else's money on people you don't know. How much are you going to care? And that's the federal government. They're spending someone else's money on people they don't know. Now imagine any other business that was this badly run that complains when you want to check the books and audit it and go through all the decisions that have been made and go through all the ledgers. The people receiving the money want to keep receiving it.
turns.zh: 是啊，就像米尔顿·弗里德曼说的：花别人的钱给不认识的人，钱花得最差。你会在意多少？联邦政府就是这样。他们花别人的钱给不认识的人。想象一下，任何其他经营这么差的企业，当你想要查账、审计、检查所有决策和账本时，它们会抱怨。收钱的人想继续收钱。

turns.en: Clearly, yes. But the reason I'm putting so much effort into this is that I think it is a very dire situation. It's not optional, basically. America's going bankrupt, and that just can't happen. It's bizarre to me that some people aren't willing to look at it correctly, to see how much chaos, waste, and fraud there is, how much could be trimmed, and how just because people have jobs doing something doesn't mean your tax dollar should pay for it.
turns.zh: 显然是的。但我投入这么多精力的原因是，我认为情况非常严峻。基本上没有选择。美国正在破产，这不能发生。让我觉得奇怪的是，有些人不愿意正确看待它，不愿意看到有多少混乱、浪费和欺诈，有多少可以削减，以及仅仅因为有人有工作做某事，并不意味着你的税款应该为此买单。

ts.sec: Social Security Fraud and Database Issues

turns.en: Yes. I mean, we found just with a basic search of the Social Security database that there were 20 million dead people marked as alive. But were they getting money?
turns.zh: 是的。我们只是对社会保障数据库进行了一次基本搜索，就发现有2000万死人被标记为活着。但他们拿到钱了吗？

turns.en: Some of them are getting money.
turns.zh: 有些人拿到了钱。

turns.en: What percentage of them?
turns.zh: 比例是多少？

turns.en: It isn't clear. We're actually trying to run the numbers. I was trying to get an answer right before the show. What it looks like is that most of the fraud is not coming from Social Security payments directly, but because they are marked as alive in the Social Security database, they can then get disability, unemployment, fake medical payments, and other things. So it looks like the fraud is a bank shot essentially: they bank shot into Social Security, they just do an 'are you alive' check, and then get fraudulent payments from every other part of the government.
turns.zh: 还不清楚。我们实际上正在计算。我在节目前正试图得到答案。看起来大多数欺诈并非直接来自社会保障支付，而是因为他们被标记为活着，然后就能获得残疾、失业、虚假医疗支付等。所以欺诈本质上是一种间接手段：他们通过社会保障做跳板，只需做一个‘你还活着吗’的检查，然后从政府其他部门获得欺诈性支付。

turns.en: Oh yeah, and this exploits the fundamental weakness in the government: the various government databases don't talk to each other, or they talk very poorly in a very limited way. So the way the system gets exploited is by taking advantage of the poor communication between the various databases in the government.
turns.zh: 哦对，这利用了政府的根本弱点：各个政府数据库不互相通信，或者通信非常差、非常有限。所以系统被利用的方式就是利用政府各数据库之间的通信不畅。

turns.en: To give you an example of what's happening in the Treasury, which is improving rapidly: the main payments computer is called PAM, like Payments Account Master database or something like that, but everyone calls it PAM. It's responsible for almost $5 trillion in payments a year, roughly a billion dollars an hour. When we came there, we looked at this payment system, and the payments have no payment categorization code and no description on the payment, basically untraceable blank checks. This is the kind of thing that if it was done as a public company, the company would be immediately delisted and the executive team would be thrown in prison. But this is just normal at the government. So we said, 'Okay, our recommendation to the Treasury and the Federal Reserve was: we need to make the payment categorization codes mandatory, not optional, and you need an explanation. We're not judging the quality of the explanation, but there should be some explanation for what this payment is for, above nothing.' That is a radical change to the system that is being implemented now. My guess is that probably saves $100 billion a year.
turns.zh: 举个例子，财政部的情况正在迅速改善：主要的支付计算机叫PAM，大概是支付账户主数据库之类的，但大家都叫它PAM。它每年负责近5万亿美元的支付，大约每小时10亿美元。我们到那里时，看了这个支付系统，发现支付没有支付分类代码，也没有描述，基本上就是无法追踪的空白支票。这种事如果发生在上市公司，公司会立即退市，管理团队会被送进监狱。但在政府里，这很正常。所以我们说：‘好的，我们给财政部和美联储的建议是：支付分类代码必须强制，不是可选的，而且需要说明。我们不评判说明的质量，但总得有个说明，说明这笔钱是干什么的，而不是什么都没有。’这是一个根本性的改变，现在正在实施。我猜这大概每年能节省1000亿美元。

turns.en: Jesus Christ. That money going, rough order magnitude, where was that money going?
turns.zh: 天哪。那笔钱，粗略数量级，流向了哪里？

turns.en: Well, this is where you get into the sort of gray boundary between waste and fraud. If money is sent to a person or organization from the government, and you didn't really deserve it but the government still sent it to you, is that waste or fraud? There's a lot of payments where someone just approved the payment, but then that payment officer changed jobs or retired or died, and the payments just keep going. It's like if you forget to pay your gym membership, but imagine it's not the gym membership, it's $20 billion a year or something. They forgot to turn it off. That's happening at scale in the government.
turns.zh: 这就进入了浪费和欺诈之间的灰色地带。如果政府把钱发给一个人或组织，你本不该得到，但政府还是发了，这是浪费还是欺诈？有很多支付，某人批准了，但那个支付官员换了工作、退休或去世了，支付却一直继续。就像你忘了取消健身房会员，但想象一下不是健身房会员，而是每年200亿美元之类的。他们忘了关掉。这种事在政府里大规模发生。

turns.en: Jesus, it's totally nuts. So what did you expect when you went in? Did you expect it would be like this?
turns.zh: 天哪，太疯狂了。你进去的时候预料到了吗？你预料到会是这样吗？

turns.en: I thought it would be bad, but I did not think it would be as bad as this. The good news is that it's a target-rich environment for saving money. It's not like if it was a very well-run ship, very efficient, it would be hard to improve. But it's not efficient, so it is actually relatively easy to improve. Let's just say it's not rocket science. I know rocket science, so it's a lot of mundane things. Some of the things are so crazy that we didn't even know to ask about them because we just assumed, like, payments out of the Treasury computer would have a payment categorization code and some explanatory note saying what the payment is for. The idea that it would be just untraceable blank checks didn't occur to us at first.
turns.zh: 我本以为会很糟，但没想到会这么糟。好消息是，这是一个节省资金的富矿。如果它是一艘管理良好的船，非常高效，那就很难改进。但它并不高效，所以实际上相对容易改进。这么说吧，这不是火箭科学。我懂火箭科学，所以很多都是平凡的事情。有些事情太疯狂了，我们甚至不知道要问，因为我们只是假设，比如财政部的支付应该有支付分类代码和说明。一开始我们没想到会是无法追踪的空白支票。

turns.en: Jesus. So anyway, this episode...
turns.zh: 天哪。那么不管怎样，这一集……

turns.en: This podcast is brought to you by NetSuite. What does the future hold for business? Ask nine experts and you'll get 10 answers. Bull market, bear market... until someone invents a crystal ball. Over 41,000 businesses are future-proofing their operations with NetSuite by Oracle, the number one cloud ERP. It brings accounting, financial management, inventory, and HR into one fluid platform. With one unified business management suite, NetSuite gives you a single source of truth, giving you the visibility and control you need to make quick, confident decisions. Plus, real-time insights and forecasting let you peer into the future with actionable data. When you can close the books in days, not weeks, you spend less time looking back and more time on what's next. Whether your company is earning millions or even hundreds of millions, NetSuite helps you respond to immediate challenges and seize your biggest opportunities. Speaking of opportunity, download the CFO's Guide to AI and Machine Learning for free at netsuite.com.
turns.zh: 本期播客由 NetSuite 赞助。商业的未来会怎样？问九个专家，你会得到十个答案。牛市、熊市……直到有人发明出水晶球。超过 41,000 家企业正在使用 Oracle 旗下的 NetSuite（排名第一的云 ERP）来为未来做准备。它将会计、财务管理、库存和人力资源整合到一个流畅的平台中。通过一个统一的业务管理套件，NetSuite 为你提供单一事实来源，让你拥有快速做出自信决策所需的可见性和控制力。此外，实时洞察和预测功能让你通过可操作的数据窥见未来。当你能在几天内（而不是几周内）完成结账时，你就能花更少时间回顾过去，更多时间规划下一步。无论你的公司盈利是数百万还是数亿美元，NetSuite 都能帮助你应对眼前的挑战并抓住最大的机遇。说到机遇，请访问 netsuite.com 免费下载《CFO 人工智能与机器学习指南》。

turns.en: So is that one of the things that accounts for that four-something trillion dollars that they don't know where it went? They don't know... I think that's probably a cumulative number. But yeah, if you add it up... Do you remember that story, Jamie? What was the story? Almost what she said, like they just didn't have accounting for it. I think it was spent on legitimate things, but we don't know what we spent it on. Well, I mean, how do you know? Obviously one cannot say it was spent legitimately if they don't know what it was spent on. That doesn't make any sense.
turns.zh: 所以，那是不是就是那四万多亿美元去向不明的原因之一？他们不知道……我想那可能是一个累计数字。但没错，如果你加起来……你还记得那个故事吗，Jamie？那个故事是什么？几乎就像她说的，他们根本没有记账。我觉得钱是花在了合法的事情上，但我们不知道花在了哪里。嗯，我的意思是，你怎么知道？显然，如果他们不知道钱花在了哪里，就不能说花得合法。这说不通。

turns.en: This is such a fascinating time because with this setup the way it is right now, with Trump back in after all that happened to him, and with you there, and with RFK Junior and Tulsi and Kash Patel, it's like this is a wild time to find out what's really going on. That's never happened before. This is nothing like the first term. In the first term, he had a bunch of neocons in the cabinet and a bunch of shady people that he didn't know, and he had to appoint all these different people. Maybe he got some bad picks. Now he's had four years to stew on it, right? And with you guys all going through this, we're getting an understanding of the government that we've literally never had before.
turns.zh: 这是一个如此迷人的时代，因为现在的局面——特朗普在经历了一切之后重新上台，还有你在这里，还有小罗伯特·F·肯尼迪、图尔西和卡什·帕特尔——这就像是一个发现真相的狂野时刻。以前从未发生过。这和第一任期完全不同。第一任期时，他的内阁里有一堆新保守派和一堆他不认识的 shady 人物，他不得不任命所有这些不同的人。也许他选了一些糟糕的人。现在他有了四年的时间来思考，对吧？而你们所有人都在经历这一切，我们正在获得对政府前所未有的理解。

turns.en: Yeah, this is a revolutionary cabinet, and maybe the most revolutionary cabinet since the first Revolution. This is not a bunch of business-as-usual types. So this is why some of the standard confirmations were quite challenging, because when you try to appoint people who are going to change the system, the system doesn't want to let them through. But it's fascinating because it's like the vampires all out themselves. Now everybody knows who the system is. If you're just lying about us and then they come and you talk on air and explain what's happening... like he's starving mothers, there's mothers that can't get food. Totally false. That's all you're hearing. No one is talking in any of these mainstream liberal talk shows. No one is talking about all this fraud and waste, because we're cutting off their graft machine. So that's what they're upset about. That's the real thing they're upset about.
turns.zh: 是的，这是一个革命性的内阁，也许是自第一次革命以来最具革命性的内阁。这不是一群按部就班的人。这就是为什么一些标准的确认过程相当具有挑战性，因为当你试图任命那些将要改变体制的人时，体制不想让他们通过。但有趣的是，就像吸血鬼都现身了。现在每个人都知道体制是谁了。如果你只是在撒谎攻击我们，然后他们来了，你在直播中解释发生了什么……比如他说他在饿死母亲，有母亲吃不上饭。完全是假的。你听到的都是这些。任何主流自由派脱口秀都没有人在谈论所有这些欺诈和浪费，因为我们正在切断他们的贪污机器。所以他们对此感到不满。这才是他们真正不满的事情。

turns.en: And if people want to know what DOGE is cutting, and I want to be clear, these are cuts that DOGE recommends to the department, and usually these recommendations are followed, but these are recommendations that are then confirmed by the department. You can see line by line what DOGE has done at doge.gov. So what if we put it on doge.gov?
turns.zh: 如果人们想知道 DOGE 在削减什么，我想明确一点，这些是 DOGE 向部门建议的削减，通常这些建议会被采纳，但这些建议随后由部门确认。你可以在 doge.gov 上逐行查看 DOGE 做了什么。那么，如果我们把它放在 doge.gov 上呢？

turns.en: So you can see everything that is being done, and there's a tracker that shows how much money has been saved. And you can look at each line item. A bunch of these sort of far-left shows will say, 'Oh, it's a constitutional crisis,' but what they won't do is point out which payments are wrong. So my challenge to them is: point out which payments are wrong. Go through it. Which of these waste fraud things are wrong? Which line? Explain that line to the public. They won't be able to. That's why you're not hearing any specifics; you're hearing stories about mothers. We can name the specifics line by line, and we got the receipts. And here's the other thing: if you post the receipts and you're only talking about the propaganda talking points, and you're not talking about the very clear fraud and waste, it's very obvious what you're doing. You're just gaslighting.
turns.zh: 所以你可以看到正在做的一切，还有一个追踪器显示省了多少钱。你可以查看每一项。那些极左的节目会说‘哦，这是宪法危机’，但他们不会指出哪些付款是错误的。所以我对他们的挑战是：指出哪些付款是错的。逐项检查。这些浪费欺诈中哪些是错的？哪一项？向公众解释那一项。他们做不到。这就是为什么你听不到任何具体内容；你听到的都是关于母亲的故事。我们可以逐项指出具体内容，而且我们有收据。还有一点：如果你贴出收据，却只谈宣传话术，而不谈明显的欺诈和浪费，那你的意图就很明显了。你只是在煤气灯操纵。

turns.en: Yeah, totally.
turns.zh: 没错，完全同意。

turns.en: Exactly. Because we say, look, in fact I've said we're going to make mistakes; we're not going to be perfect. So if we make a mistake, we'll quickly fix it. We need to act fast to stop wasting billions of dollars of taxpayer money. But if we make a mistake, we'll reverse it quickly. It's also this interesting narrative that you shouldn't have access to this social security information, as if no one's had access to it before. As if the Biden Administration in 2023 had 53 people, some of them students, that had access to all this stuff. As it is, there are tens of thousands of federal employees that have access already to the system. Anyone from DOGE has to go through the same vetting process that those federal employees went through. So there's not some unvetted random situation. If, for example, there's a security clearance needed, the DOGE person has to have that same security clearance. So there's no reduction in security. But I mean, obviously, the vast numbers of social security numbers have leaked onto the internet. People have hacked the government systems multiple times. Vast amounts of public information has been hacked and dumped onto the internet. There's a guy at the IRS that leaked a half a million tax returns just a few years ago on purpose.
turns.zh: 没错。因为我们说，实际上我说过我们会犯错；我们不会完美。所以如果我们犯了错，我们会迅速修正。我们需要快速行动，停止浪费数十亿美元的纳税人资金。但如果犯了错，我们会迅速撤销。还有一种有趣的说法，说你不应该访问这些社保信息，好像以前没人能访问一样。好像2023年拜登政府有53个人，其中一些是学生，能访问所有这些信息。实际上，已经有数万名联邦雇员可以访问这个系统。任何来自DOGE的人都必须经过与那些联邦雇员相同的审查程序。所以不存在未经审查的随机情况。例如，如果需要安全许可，DOGE的人必须拥有同样的安全许可。所以安全性没有降低。但显然，大量社保号码已经泄露到互联网上。人们多次入侵政府系统。大量公共信息被黑客窃取并发布到网上。几年前，国税局有个人故意泄露了50万份纳税申报单。

turns.en: Yeah, for what reason?
turns.zh: 是啊，出于什么原因？

turns.en: For political reasons. He wanted to get Trump, and maybe me and a few others. So he stole like 500,000 tax returns. Not a few, like a lot.
turns.zh: 出于政治原因。他想搞特朗普，也许还有我和其他几个人。所以他偷了大约50万份纳税申报单。不是少数，是很多。

turns.en: Jesus Christ. Yeah, I remember that story now. You just read about it online; it's a real thing.
turns.zh: 天哪。是啊，我现在想起那个故事了。你只是在网上读到过，但这是真事。

turns.en: So these are the narratives. The narrative that you shouldn't have access to social security. The other narrative is that starving people are going to die and women aren't going to be pregnant and not have nutrients for their babies. That's all you're hearing. And that's the only thing they can say. But they can point to the line item, right? So they can't say, 'Well, this is the thing where the nutrients for pregnant mothers were stopped.' They can't point to that because we didn't. It's a lie.
turns.zh: 所以这些就是那些叙事。你不应该访问社保的叙事。另一个叙事是饥饿的人会死，女性无法怀孕，婴儿没有营养。你听到的都是这些。这是他们唯一能说的。但他们可以指出具体项目，对吧？所以他们不能说‘这就是孕妇营养被停止的项目’。他们指不出来，因为我们没有做。这是谎言。

turns.en: What's fascinating to me is how much the mainstream media is in line with the very specific talking points, and how little you have Fox News. You essentially have Fox News on television; it's like the only one that is pointing out the ridiculous fraud and waste.
turns.zh: 让我着迷的是，主流媒体在多大程度上与这些非常具体的宣传口径保持一致，而福克斯新闻却很少。电视上基本上只有福克斯新闻在指出这些荒谬的欺诈和浪费。

turns.en: Yeah, I know you saw the Jeff Bezos thing in the Washington Post. They're going to stop all the wacky editorials and limit that stuff to wealth and personal freedom or something along those lines.
turns.zh: 是啊，我知道你看到了《华盛顿邮报》关于杰夫·贝佐斯的事。他们将停止所有古怪的社论，并将其限制在财富和个人自由之类的范围内。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. So I mean, I think it makes sense because he's just talking about the opinion pieces, not the regular journalism. The regular journalism stays the same.
turns.zh: 是啊，是啊。所以我觉得这有道理，因为他只是谈论观点文章，而不是常规新闻。常规新闻保持不变。

turns.en: Well, it's a detriment to their business. I mean, you're seeing over and over again that people just don't want to hear all this from these people anymore. It's like you're caught in an outdated version of the virus, and everybody else already has the immunity to that virus. You need a new mind virus; the one you're pushing doesn't work anymore. It's too crazy.
turns.zh: 嗯，这对他们的业务不利。我的意思是，你一次又一次地看到，人们不再想听这些人的这些言论了。这就像你感染了一种过时的病毒，而其他所有人已经对这种病毒有了免疫力。你需要一种新的思想病毒；你正在推的那个已经不管用了。它太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah, the whole thing is very crazy. I mean, the media is incredibly partisan. I mean, they're not—almost all the media is left-shifted. So it's kind of weird. If you talk to somebody who gets all their information from what I call legacy media, they're living in a different world than if they are listening to your podcast or getting news from X. It's kind of wild. There are a lot of people that I talk to that I have to ask, 'Where did you hear that?'
turns.zh: 是啊，整件事非常疯狂。我的意思是，媒体极度党派化。几乎所有媒体都左倾。所以有点奇怪。如果你和那些从我所称的‘传统媒体’获取所有信息的人交谈，他们生活在另一个世界，而如果他们听你的播客或从X获取新闻，则完全不同。这有点疯狂。我认识的很多人，我都得问‘你在哪儿听说的？’

turns.en: Yeah, but I mean, the Associated Press, which I call Associated Propaganda, the AP, they ran an international news story saying that DOGE fired air traffic controllers. But we didn't fire any air traffic controllers at all. In fact, we're trying to hire air traffic controllers, not fire them.
turns.zh: 是啊，但我的意思是，美联社，我称之为‘联合宣传’，他们发了一篇国际新闻报道说DOGE解雇了空中交通管制员。但我们根本没有解雇任何空中交通管制员。事实上，我们正在努力招聘空中交通管制员，而不是解雇他们。

turns.en: Yeah, I saw that you made a tweet about it. What do you call it now? Do you call it a post? You can't call it a tweet though. Do you call it a tweet accidentally?
turns.zh: 是啊，我看到你发了一条推文。现在你管它叫什么？叫帖子？不过你不能叫推文了。你是不小心叫成推文了吗？

turns.en: I don't know. Listen, but if somebody puts up a two-hour-long video, that's not a tweet; it's a post. Good point.
turns.zh: 我不知道。听着，但如果有人发了一个两小时的视频，那不是推文；那是帖子。说得好。

turns.en: Yeah, for sure.
turns.zh: 是啊，确实。

turns.en: So, but I posted about it, saying that we need highly qualified air traffic controllers. If you retired, if you would consider doing it again, we could use you. A lot of really qualified air traffic controllers were pushed out because of DEI stuff. Not to be blunt, but a bunch of really good, talented old white guys were pushed out. It's not cool. So we have a talent shortage in air traffic control because of DEI and not hiring people on merit. Which is so crazy. I think we should not put public safety at risk because of some demented philosophy. Somebody made a post today about it infiltrating the NSA. Did you see any of that? That was some gnarly stuff.
turns.zh: 所以，但我发帖说了，我们需要高素质的空中交通管制员。如果你退休了，如果你愿意再干一次，我们可以用你。很多非常合格的空中交通管制员因为DEI（多元、公平、包容）而被赶走。不客气地说，一群非常优秀、有才华的老白男被赶走了。这不好。所以我们因为DEI和不按能力招聘而面临空中交通管制人才短缺。这太疯狂了。我认为我们不应该因为某种疯狂的理念而危及公共安全。今天有人发帖说它渗透进了NSA。你看到那些了吗？那是一些很糟糕的东西。

turns.en: Yeah, crazy. What they did—it started off as just this sort of fringe thing, and people would meet up, then it completely infiltrated the...
turns.zh: 是啊，疯狂。他们做的——一开始只是这种边缘的东西，人们会见面，然后它完全渗透了……

ts.sec: NSA Scandal: Sexually Explicit Chat Rooms

turns.en: They were spending all their time there, like 400 people or something, in some chat sex chat room with some extremely demented stuff.
turns.zh: 他们把所有时间都花在那上面，大概有400人左右，在一些聊天室里搞极其变态的东西。

turns.en: I know, I saw it. I'll send it to you, Jamie, because it's so cooky.
turns.zh: 我知道，我看到了。我会发给你，Jamie，因为这太离谱了。

turns.en: This is the NSA. I thought the NSA was all about information and spying on national threats.
turns.zh: 这可是NSA。我以为NSA只负责情报和监视国家威胁。

turns.en: So, more than 100 intelligence staffers will be fired over sexually explicit texts in NSA chat rooms, Gabbert says.
turns.zh: 所以，Gabbert说，超过100名情报人员因在NSA聊天室发送色情短信将被解雇。

turns.en: A top intelligence official told Waters that the workers in question were brazen, using an NSA platform intended for professional use to conduct this horrific behavior.
turns.zh: 一位高级情报官员告诉Waters，涉事员工厚颜无耻地使用本应用于专业工作的NSA平台进行这种可怕的行为。

turns.en: What exactly is the behavior? What does the article say?
turns.zh: 具体是什么行为？文章里怎么说？

turns.en: It says employees participated in obscene, pornographic, and sexually explicit chat rooms. Your tax dollars at work.
turns.zh: 文章说员工参与了淫秽、色情和露骨的聊天室。你的税金就这样被用掉了。

turns.en: It was all LGBTQ stuff, a lot of transition stuff. I definitely saved it.
turns.zh: 全是LGBTQ内容，很多变性相关的东西。我肯定保存了。

turns.en: But the point is, it had infiltrated the organization. It's not what they should be talking about at all. They're supposed to be protecting the country.
turns.zh: 但关键是，这已经渗透到了组织内部。这根本不是他们该讨论的内容。他们应该保护国家。

turns.en: People were talking about how they spend half their time in these meetings, constantly having to attend these things where they talk about these issues. What are you doing?
turns.zh: 人们说他们一半时间都花在这些会议上，不断参加这些讨论。你们到底在干什么？

turns.en: If you have a problem with someone that's discriminatory, get rid of that person. That's it. Problem's over.
turns.zh: 如果有人存在歧视问题，就开除那个人。就这么简单。问题就解决了。

turns.en: If you have someone who's homophobic in your business, openly homophobic, you can't work here. That's not cool. That's it. You don't have to have meetings constantly promoting this. You're not going to change someone's opinion by berating them over and over again.
turns.zh: 如果你的公司里有恐同的人，公开恐同，那就不能在这里工作。这不酷。就这样。你不需要不断开会宣传这些。反复斥责不会改变别人的看法。

turns.en: A work environment should be professional, where they're getting the job done that they're paid to do. It shouldn't be about getting paid for bizarre sexcapades.
turns.zh: 工作环境应该是专业的，完成他们受雇做的工作。不应该为了古怪的性冒险而拿工资。

turns.en: It's fascinating that the virus was so strong that it made it into the NSA. You would think those are hard-nosed people. The CIA too, they were in there too, which is bananas.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，这种病毒如此强大，竟然渗透进了NSA。你会以为那里都是强硬的人。CIA也卷入了，这太疯狂了。

turns.en: You would think the same thing: hard-nosed, tough people doing hard work who can spy on you whenever they want and get revenge on you whenever they want. Pretty wild.
turns.zh: 你会以为同样如此：强硬、坚韧的人做艰苦的工作，可以随时监视你，随时报复你。相当疯狂。

turns.en: They exist when the president leaves; they stay. People move around, you stay a part of the organization for your entire career. You get deeply entrenched in their system and how things work, who's back to rub, who's a bad guy, who's a good guy, who's on our side, who's not. It's scary actually.
turns.zh: 总统离职时他们还在；他们留下来。人们来来去去，但你整个职业生涯都是组织的一部分。你深深嵌入他们的系统，了解运作方式，谁该巴结，谁是坏人，谁是好人，谁站在我们这边，谁不是。其实很可怕。

ts.sec: Epstein Files Delay and Evidence Concerns

turns.en: So, is that what's taken so long with these Epstein files? What's up with that?
turns.zh: 所以，这就是爱泼斯坦文件拖了这么久的原因吗？到底怎么回事？

turns.en: It's like Lucy and the football with Charlie Brown when she always pulls that football away. It's the same thing. They keep telling us they're going to release it. Day one, oh day one. We have a serious case of no one being arrested. O phobia, you know?
turns.zh: 就像露西和查理·布朗的橄榄球，她总是把球抽走。一样的事。他们一直说要公布。第一天，哦第一天。我们面临一个严重问题：没人被捕。O恐惧症，你知道吗？

turns.en: There's also this real fear that someone's destroying the evidence. You keep hearing these stories, unsubstantiated stories, of FBI people where the evidence... I mean, the guy had tons of videos and recordings. He had all sorts of things. There's a mountain of evidence. So where's that mountain?
turns.zh: 还有一种真正的恐惧，就是有人在销毁证据。你不断听到这些故事，未经证实的，关于FBI人员……我的意思是，那家伙有大量视频和录音。他有各种东西。证据堆积如山。那么那座山在哪？

turns.en: What would be the reason why they would agree to not put it out? There would have to be something in it for them. Some sort of financial entanglement, some sort of relationship with the people on that list, that they can provide a value big enough for you to not release it, or slow release it, or hope you can get away with putting out some redacted files that don't show anything.
turns.zh: 他们同意不公布的原因是什么？肯定对他们有好处。某种财务纠葛，与名单上的人有某种关系，以至于他们能提供足够大的价值，让你不公布，或缓慢公布，或希望你能侥幸发布一些不显示任何内容的删节文件。

turns.en: This is only redacted, redacted, redacted. Only stage one. Don't worry, the real stuff's coming. That doesn't make any sense. Why wouldn't you just release it all? What could possibly be worth protecting in there?
turns.zh: 这只是删节版，删节版，删节版。只是第一阶段。别担心，真正的东西会来的。这毫无道理。为什么不全部公布？里面有什么值得保护的？

turns.en: I think I've got probably the same information. I'm just reading what's the latest thing on X. I'm just looking at my X feed and I'm like, yeah, it's a real page turner. I thought we were going to get some revelations. It was like big binders full of stuff. There's got to be something in there.
turns.zh: 我想我可能得到了同样的信息。我只是在看X上的最新消息。我刷着X动态，心想，这真是引人入胜。我以为我们会得到一些爆料。就像装满东西的大活页夹。里面肯定有料。

turns.en: All those people that were given a copy of it were waving around like they got the Willy Wonka ticket. And what happened? Nothing. So Laura Loomer released it online. I think I'm watching her. She's not very pleased about this.
turns.zh: 所有拿到副本的人都像拿到了威利·旺卡的金票一样挥舞着。然后呢？什么都没发生。所以Laura Loomer在网上公布了。我想我在看她。她对此很不满。

turns.en: Does anybody find anything in there that's interesting? No. It's all old stuff from 2015. What the hell is going on?
turns.zh: 有人发现里面有什么有趣的东西吗？没有。全是2015年的旧东西。到底怎么回事？

turns.en: Apparently they discovered a whole bunch of stuff at the Southern District of New York. And I think Pam Bondi is actually great and Kash Patel is great, but they just got there. They're in a hostile environment, not a friendly one.
turns.zh: 显然他们在纽约南区发现了一大堆东西。我觉得Pam Bondi和Kash Patel其实很棒，但他们刚上任。他们身处敌对环境，不是友好环境。

turns.en: It's like if you suddenly got put in captain of a ship but the crew was previously your enemy. The entire crew was previously your enemy. You're telling them, 'Give me evidence.' The crew doesn't want to give you the evidence. They were your mortal enemies just a moment ago. You just got there.
turns.zh: 就像你突然被任命为一艘船的船长，但船员以前是你的敌人。整个船员以前都是你的敌人。你告诉他们，‘把证据给我。’船员不想给你证据。他们刚才还是你的死敌。你刚到。

turns.en: I think we got to give the Attorney General and the new Director of the FBI a little bit of slack here because they literally just got there.
turns.zh: 我认为我们得给司法部长和FBI新局长一点宽容，因为他们真的刚到。

turns.en: I think so too. But hey, don't say you're going to release it on day one then. You shouldn't have said that. And don't say you got a big drop coming tomorrow and then it's something that's been around forever. It's disappointing.
turns.zh: 我也这么认为。但是，嘿，那就别说第一天就公布。你不该那么说。也别说明天有大料，结果却是老掉牙的东西。真令人失望。

turns.en: What the hell? And where's the JFK files? Where are those? Let them go. Did they release anything on that front?
turns.zh: 搞什么？肯尼迪文件呢？那些在哪？公布吧。他们在这方面公布什么了吗？

turns.en: I don't know what's going on. It can't be anything that's gotten to me yet. So if nothing's gotten to me yet, it can't be significant. If there's conspiracy evidence, someone's going to send it. Tim Dillon's going to text me, 100%. Tim Dillon, Dave Smith, someone's going to send my way. So it hasn't been released yet.
turns.zh: 我不知道怎么回事。肯定还没到我手上。如果还没到我手上，那就不重要。如果有阴谋证据，肯定会有人发给我。Tim Dillon会发短信给我，100%。Tim Dillon、Dave Smith，肯定会有人发给我。所以还没公布。

turns.en: You would find out. And here's the real question: what could even be in there at this point that they haven't cleared out? If you've got paperwork from 1963, what is in there?
turns.zh: 你会知道的。真正的问题是：到了现在，里面还有什么他们没清理掉的？如果你有1963年的文件，里面有什么？

turns.en: Still, what is in there that could possibly be incriminating? That supposedly Trump said, 'If you saw what they showed me, you wouldn't release it either.' Okay, what the hell does that mean? I haven't seen it. So Kash has? Yes, he said he's seen it all. Can he just post it to his X account or something? That sounds like an Elon move.
turns.zh: 但里面到底有什么可能构成罪证的东西？据说特朗普说过：‘如果你看到他们给我看的东西，你也不会公布。’这到底是什么意思？我没看到过。所以卡什看到了？是的，他说他全都看过了。他能不能直接发到他的X账号上？这听起来像是埃隆会干的事。

turns.en: I don't think he can. He's the director of the FBI. I think he has to go through proper channels. Does he have the channel? Yeah, but there are rules. He sounds like Trump. He needs an executive order.
turns.zh: 我觉得他不能。他是FBI局长，得走正规渠道。他有渠道吗？有，但有规矩。他听起来像特朗普。他需要一道行政命令。

turns.en: What about 'I am the storm'? I mean, what channels? He is the channel. Well, again, imagine just getting to the hull or the deck of the ship and you're the captain. Now you have to figure out who's running things, who's doing this, where everything is. Just getting anything done. Like you just joined as captain of a ship where the crew hates your guts. They were your enemy. They're strongly opposed to anything you want to do. You're trying to give them orders and expose them. They don't want to be exposed. People working there are probably part of this problem.
turns.zh: 那‘我就是风暴’呢？什么渠道？他就是渠道。再想象一下，你刚登上船体或甲板，你是船长。现在你得搞清楚谁在管事，谁在干这些，东西都在哪。什么事都办不成。就像你刚当上船长，但船员都恨你入骨。他们曾是你的敌人。他们强烈反对你想做的任何事。你想给他们下命令，想揭露他们。他们不想被揭露。在那里工作的人很可能就是问题的一部分。

turns.en: I was reading on X that Comey's daughter is the lead prosecutor in the Southern District of New York. Did you read that? Yes. So obviously there's a bit of an entanglement there. What if there's something that puts her dad in a bad light? She'd hit that delete button.
turns.zh: 我在X上看到科米的女儿是纽约南区首席检察官。你看到了吗？看到了。所以显然有点牵连。万一有什么东西让她爸难堪呢？她肯定会按删除键。

turns.en: Did you see General Flynn? He was on a podcast and spoke directly to James Comey. He said, 'Jim, you're going to jail unless you give up someone deeper than you, and you know who that is.' That is wild.
turns.zh: 你看到弗林将军了吗？他上了个播客，直接对詹姆斯·科米说：‘吉姆，你要进监狱了，除非你供出比你更深的人，你知道我说的是谁。’这太疯狂了。

turns.en: I think the former director of the FBI might be really in that deep. He actually was doing some evil, corrupt things while running the FBI. There seems to be some very shady stuff going on. It definitely happened in the '60s. Everybody admits the FBI killed Black Panthers. They did a lot of stuff we know the government did back in the day.
turns.zh: 我觉得前FBI局长可能真的陷得很深。他在掌管FBI时确实干了一些邪恶腐败的事。似乎有一些非常见不得人的勾当。这种事在60年代肯定发生过。大家都承认FBI杀了黑豹党成员。他们干了很多我们知道的政府当年干的事。

turns.en: Why don't we just data dump the files? Go in there, take photos of all the papers, presumably paper, and just post it online. Let the chips fall where they may. Is it presumably in some filing cabinet somewhere? I don't know. Where is the magic filing cabinet? How are they hiding it? Who has access to it? This is what I was hoping for on day one. Obviously it's taking a lot longer than that.
turns.zh: 为什么不直接把文件数据全部公开？进去把所有文件（大概是纸质文件）拍下来，直接发到网上。让结果顺其自然。是不是在某个文件柜里？我不知道。那个神奇的文件柜在哪？他们怎么藏的？谁有权限？我本来希望第一天就能这样。显然这需要更长的时间。

turns.en: Let's say you were made director of the FBI. That's what's crazy. Literally, what is he doing now? He's one of the big dogs of the FBI, but he's a Secret Service guy. A legit guy. But people think of him as a Fox News guy, just like Pete Hegseth. They don't want to think about his distinguished military career. They want to say, 'Oh, that Fox News guy.' Director, deputy director of the FBI. Dan is hardcore. If it's reasonably findable, I think he's going to find it. This is between him and Kash. I think they're going to get stuff.
turns.zh: 假设你被任命为FBI局长。这太疯狂了。他现在在干什么？他是FBI的大人物之一，但他是特勤局出身。一个正经人。但人们把他当成福克斯新闻的人，就像皮特·赫格塞斯一样。他们不想考虑他杰出的军旅生涯。他们只想说：‘哦，那个福克斯新闻的人。’FBI局长、副局长。丹很硬核。如果东西合理可寻，我觉得他会找到。这是他和卡什之间的事。我觉得他们会找到东西。

turns.en: How crazy would it be if they couldn't? If everybody shuts their mouth and covers their ass? An FBI computer where you type in the search... I just think about the basic mechanism. It's either in a filing cabinet in paper, with progressive levels of security. Open this door, you need a pass for this level. Level unlocked, and there's a laptop. You have to plug it in. It's in a SCIF.
turns.zh: 如果他们找不到，那得有多疯狂？如果所有人都闭嘴、互相包庇呢？一台FBI电脑，你输入搜索……我只是想基本机制。要么是纸质文件柜，有层层安全级别。打开这扇门，你需要这一级的通行证。解锁一级，还有一台笔记本电脑。你得插上电。它在SCIF（敏感隔离信息设施）里。

turns.en: This is why I think a tour of Fort Knox would be awesome. A live tour of Fort Knox. We can actually see if the gold is there or not. They say it is. Is it real, or did somebody spray-paint some lead? Imagine if it's not all there. Some of it's missing. Where did it go? What if a lot of it's missing? Half of it's missing? How do we even know? The last time they let someone look at it was decades ago. The last formal audit was in the '50s.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我觉得参观诺克斯堡会很棒。现场直播参观诺克斯堡。我们就能亲眼看到黄金还在不在。他们说在。是真的，还是有人把铅块喷了漆？想象一下如果黄金不全在那里。有些不见了。去哪了？如果很多都不见了呢？一半不见了？我们怎么知道？上一次让人查看是几十年前。上一次正式审计是在50年代。

turns.en: Think about all the other stuff you pointed out: the checks that go out, the NGO payments, Social Security. Apply that to the gold. I just want to emphasize the sheer madness of the government. They have magic money computers. All the checks never bounce for the federal government. You don't have the normal corrective mechanism you'd have for a company or an individual. The checks always clear. The net result is inflation, which is effectively a tax on everyone. The Defense Department hasn't passed an audit in seven years.
turns.zh: 想想你指出的所有其他东西：开出的支票、非政府组织付款、社会保障。把同样的问题套用到黄金上。我只是想强调政府的疯狂。他们有魔法般的金钱计算机。联邦政府的支票永远不会跳票。你没有公司或个人那种正常的纠正机制。支票总是能兑现。最终结果是通货膨胀，实际上是对每个人征税。国防部已经七年没有通过审计了。

turns.en: You'd have to be Chuck Norris to get the Defense Department to pass an audit. That's the level of skill you need. That's what's so insane. If you bring it back to the idea that it's a business, this would never be tolerated in any functional business. The Pentagon's accounting errors, the stuff they lose in the couch cushions, is 20 to 30 billion dollars a year. They don't know where it went. It's gone. It's so insane.
turns.zh: 你得是查克·诺里斯才能让国防部通过审计。你需要那种级别的技能。这就是为什么这么疯狂。如果你把它当成一家企业，这在任何正常企业里都是不可容忍的。五角大楼的会计错误，他们丢在沙发垫子里的钱，每年有200到300亿美元。他们不知道钱去哪了。没了。太疯狂了。

turns.en: That's why I said even simple things like requiring outgoing payments from the Treasury computer to have a payment code, a comment about what the payment is for, and someone to call about the payment, would have a very powerful effect in stopping wasteful outflows and fraud.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我说，即使是简单的要求，比如财政部电脑的对外付款必须有付款代码、付款用途说明和联系人，也会对阻止浪费性支出和欺诈产生非常强大的效果。

turns.en: Here's another way to look at this. Imagine if there are people like you and the DOGE team out there. Imagine if one of them works for an organization like USAID or any other organization, has this understanding of how much fuckery is involved, but has evil intentions, and they're entwined in this system for decades. They've built a career and all the entanglements that come with it.
turns.zh: 还有另一种看法。想象一下，如果世界上有像你和DOGE团队这样的人。想象一下，如果其中一个人为像美国国际开发署这样的组织工作，深知其中有多少猫腻，但心怀恶意，并且在这个系统中纠缠了几十年。他们建立了职业生涯和随之而来的所有牵连。

turns.zh: 而且他们开始四处活动，你大概能轻松做到。按照你的描述，如果你是个资深业内人士，一直待在里面，知道一切怎么运作，又很聪明，你就能搞出点名堂，可能还能跟你认识的、正在组建非政府组织的人合作——嘿，我们一起干吧。对，对，这就是你面临的阻力。我觉得这是史上最大的骗局，你从没见过这么大的骗局，人类历史上最大的骗局。是的，哇，我觉得你说得对。对，还有别的吗？大概是个万亿美元的骗局，从来没有过万亿美元的骗局。你知道，这可不是你一开始想做的事，这不是你的职业抱负，这简直是我能想象到的最荒谬的结果。实际上，狗狗币最初也是一种 meme 币，对吧？它就是一个关于 meme 和狗的玩笑加密货币。嗯，有趣的是，缩写最后竟然完美契合。其实我本来想叫“政府效率委员会”，一个很无聊的名字，然后网上的人说，不，应该叫“政府效率部”。我说，你说得对，当然，当然。这更像是模拟世界的证据，那个小吉祥物是一只可爱的狗，一个 meme 币。这个 meme 币现在可能值很多钱，对吧？每次你发推文，它可能就涨了。对，整个 meme 币的事情太疯狂了。人们把真金白银投进这些币里，然后你可以拉高出货，就像赌场一样。我不知道，对，完全是赌博。人们就是玩“博傻游戏”和“抢椅子”，最后坐下的人输。不知怎么的，它居然还是合法的。我觉得没多少人……这有点像你去赌场，如果你指望在赌场赢钱，你就是个傻瓜。所以我认为如果你指望靠 meme 币赢钱，那你就是愚蠢。对，对，你不可能靠 meme 币赢钱。但如果你想玩一玩，可以小赌一把，跟着波动赚点小钱或亏点小钱，找点乐子。但别把毕生积蓄投进 meme 币。最奇怪的是拉高出货，人们总是上当，然后惊讶地发现有人拉高出货。你在干什么？你指望我也拉高出货？我指望赚走所有钱，不敢相信我被坑了。对，奇怪的是它居然还合法。赌场也合法，人们在赌场输钱，但你不能操纵赌场，而拉高出货可以操纵。对，我想是的，你可以搞真正的金字塔骗局。要我说，政府本身就是一个大金字塔骗局。对，你可以说社会保障是有史以来最大的庞氏骗局。解释一下。

turns.en: Oh, so well people pay into Social Security and the money goes out of Social Security immediately, but the obligation for Social Security is your entire retirement career. So you're paying with your... if you look at the future obligations of Social Security, it far exceeds the tax revenue. Have you ever looked at the debt clock? Yes. There's our present day debt, but then there's our future obligations. So when you look at the future obligations of Social Security, the actual national debt is like double what people think it is because of the future obligations. Ah, so basically people are living way longer than expected, and there are fewer babies being born. So you have more people who are retired and live for a long time and get retirement payments.
