Yeah, mentally ill dude with a wig on. Say that. Yes, but NBC even, you're looking at the picture, a woman? Yo, this is a dude with a wrong jawline, okay? He's wearing woman's face. It's a buff dude. Yeah, it's a buff dude wearing woman's face. I mean, come on, that's not a woman. Yeah, and they're like, watch out for disinformation. I'm like, what are you talking about? It's so crazy. This is just more evidence of the virus, though, right? It killed objectivity, killed reality, and it demanded strict adherence or you were attacked. Yeah, yeah, any questioning of it would result in being ostracized.
turns.zh: 不，你看，我是说在旧金山，你可以在市政厅前在市长车上拉屎的同时注射海洛因，好吧，什么都不会发生。但如果你戴着MAGA帽子走在街上，你会被攻击。是的，这太疯狂了。太疯狂了。而且这太奥威尔了，一顶写着'让美国再次伟大'的帽子会引起人们的暴力反应。难道你不是美国人吗？这对每个人来说不都是好事吗？让美国再次伟大。但因为它是唐纳德·特朗普和那顶红帽子，你会因为戴那顶红帽子而被喷辣椒水。他们会通过殴打你来让美国变得更糟。所以他们在做一件邪恶的事，在美国进行暴力攻击，因为你想要让美国再次伟大。我是说，这就像书里的场景。看起来不可能那么荒谬。还记得当'所有生命都重要'会让你被解雇吗？这太疯狂了。人们因为说了'所有生命都重要'而被解雇，这是非常合理的话。这有多合理？是的，这基本上就是说每个人都很重要。但这不是你应该说的。你必须说'黑人的命也是命'，当然他们很重要。如果你说'所有生命都重要'，每个人都很重要。是的，但色盲社会的想法在2012年左右被完全抛弃了。我是说，我大概可以追溯到枪表情符号被削弱的时候，你知道吗？它什么时候变成了水枪？那是几年前，大概是2016年，我想。是吗？是的，它变成了水枪。你能在X上把它带回来吗？是的，不，现在如果你在X上使用枪表情符号，苹果会坚持它是水枪，然后X应用会把它变回1911手枪。哦，真的吗？是的，哦，那太好了。所以你可以实际拥有一把1911手枪。我们在应用内撤销了苹果的更改。哦，真搞笑。真搞笑，那个东西那么冒犯，枪，还有孕妇男，这两个都让我受不了。我喜欢那个梗，就是告诉你你听到的是虚假信息的人，正是做了孕妇男表情符号的人。是的，想想吧。嗯，还有，同样的人说一个女人袭击了特斯拉工厂。是的，那个女人，是个男的。是个男的，很明显是男的，非常大块头，有精神病的男的。是的，有精神病的男的戴着假发。说出来。是的，但NBC甚至，你看照片，一个女人？哟，这是个下巴线条不对的男的，好吗？他戴着女人的脸。是个肌肉男。是的，是个肌肉男戴着女人的脸。我是说，拜托，那不是女人。是的，他们还说，小心虚假信息。我说，你在说什么？太疯狂了。这不过是病毒的更多证据，对吧？它杀死了客观性，杀死了现实，它要求严格服从，否则你就会受到攻击。是的，是的，任何质疑都会导致被排斥。

turns.en: Do you like, what kind of responsibility do you like, knowing that if you didn't take over Twitter and turn it into X, if that didn't happen, I really think the world's a very different place right now. Like, how long have you owned it for? A couple years. Basically, imagine a couple years of it being run the way it was run before, and probably accelerating. My account would have been suspended long ago, for sure. Yeah, for sure. Just for the disinformation. Yeah. It would have been, Trump would have never come back. Alex Jones would have definitely never been back, definitely not. No, no. So, yeah. Does that weigh on you? Like, I would feel like that would be a heavy responsibility. Yeah. I mean, I'm just trying to keep civilization.
turns.zh: 你喜欢吗，什么样的责任？知道如果你没有接管Twitter并把它变成X，如果那没有发生，我真的认为世界现在会非常不同。你拥有它多久了？几年。基本上，想象一下它像以前那样运行几年，而且可能还在加速。我的账号早就被禁了，肯定的。是的，肯定的。就因为虚假信息。是的。特朗普永远不会回来。亚历克斯·琼斯肯定永远不会回来，绝对不会。不，不。所以，是的。这让你感到沉重吗？我会觉得那是一个沉重的责任。是的。我是说，我只是在努力维持文明。

turns.en: I think we at least want to build a city on Mars and become a multiplanet civilization, which I think would be incredibly important in ensuring the long-term survival of civilization.
turns.zh: 我认为我们至少要在火星上建造一座城市，成为多行星文明，这对确保文明的长期存续至关重要。

turns.en: Are you still rescuing those people that are stuck in the space station?
turns.zh: 你还在救那些被困在空间站的人吗？

turns.en: Yeah, that's coming up in a couple weeks, I think.
turns.zh: 是的，大概几周后吧。

turns.en: Whoa, they've been up there for how long? Jamie, they were supposed to be there for a couple days, right?
turns.zh: 哇，他们在上面待了多久？Jamie，他们本来只该待几天的，对吧？

turns.en: I think actually probably like four weeks. Oh, they were supposed to be up there for like eight days, yeah, and they've been up there for like I think eight months, so a little longer than expected.
turns.zh: 实际上大概四周了。哦，他们本来只该待八天，结果已经待了八个月，比预期长了一点。

turns.en: What is it going to be like for those people when they get back? They're going to be a wreck for a long time, right?
turns.zh: 他们回来后会怎么样？会很久都恢复不了吧？

turns.en: Yeah, as long as you stay up there, you get increased bone loss. So it ended up being like this political football, a hotly contested topic. We offered to bring them back early, but this offer was rejected by the Biden Administration for political reasons.
turns.zh: 是的，在太空待久了骨质流失会加剧。所以这成了政治皮球，争议很大。我们提出早点接他们回来，但拜登政府出于政治原因拒绝了。

turns.en: That's so crazy. There's no way that they're going to make anyone who's supporting Trump look good.
turns.zh: 太疯狂了。他们绝不可能让任何支持特朗普的人好看。

turns.en: Wow, what do you think they would have done if they had won?
turns.zh: 哇，你觉得如果他们赢了会怎么做？

turns.en: Well, get those people back. No, they can only get them back with a SpaceX spacecraft. But they pushed the return date past the inauguration date. Wow, so they would have let you do it, but after the wow.
turns.zh: 嗯，把那些人接回来。不，他们只能用SpaceX的飞船接他们回来。但他们把返回日期推到了就职典礼之后。哇，所以他们会让你做，但要在那之后。

turns.en: And so it would have been them authorizing you?
turns.zh: 所以会是他们授权你？

turns.en: There isn't anyone else to do it. NASA can't get them. The only SpaceX Dragon spacecraft is the only one that is considered safe enough to bring them back. NASA concluded that the Boeing spacecraft was not safe, so that's why they're stuck there.
turns.zh: 没有其他人能做。NASA接不了他们。只有SpaceX的龙飞船被认为足够安全能接他们回来。NASA认定波音的飞船不安全，所以他们才被困在那里。

turns.en: Holy yeah. And you can't ask Russia to help, that would be awkward.
turns.zh: 天哪。你也不能找俄罗斯帮忙，那会很尴尬。

turns.en: A little bit, yeah. It'd be a nice thing if they did. They said 'guys, we'll help you.' I think for enough money they would.
turns.zh: 有点。如果他们帮忙就好了。他们会说‘伙计们，我们来帮你们’。我觉得给够钱他们会帮的。

turns.en: You think so?
turns.zh: 你这么想？

turns.en: Yeah, but they would obviously treat it as a propaganda victory and charge crazy money. It's just disgusting that they would use that as a political tool.
turns.zh: 是的，但他们显然会把它当作宣传胜利，并收取天价费用。他们把它当作政治工具，真恶心。

turns.en: Yeah. The Biden Administration was also suing SpaceX. They had this massive lawsuit against SpaceX for not hiring asylum seekers. So people say 'oh, Elon's making it up, this administration wasn't against SpaceX.' I'm like, bro, the Department of Justice had a massive lawsuit against SpaceX for not hiring asylum seekers, even though it is illegal for us to hire anyone who is not a permanent resident. So it is both: there's a law that says you have to hire asylum seekers, but there's also a law that says anyone hired by a rocket company, which is an advanced weapons technology, must be a permanent resident. And an asylum seeker is not a permanent resident. So it is both legal and illegal to hire asylum seekers. So why would the Biden Administration launch a massive lawsuit? This is public information, it's not like my imagination. Why would they launch such a massive lawsuit against SpaceX? They're extremely antagonistic. It just doesn't make any sense that that could ever even get past the first day of someone looking at it. If it's both illegal and you're trying to enforce it, you can't enforce it. Yes, this is an advanced weapons company. This is crazy. It should be thrown out. There's the International Traffic in Arms Regulations, a law that ensures only permanent residents of the United States can work at advanced weapons companies. Rockets are advanced weapons. The same is true for nuclear, bioweapons, or something like that. Because if someone were to work at SpaceX and then go to North Korea or Iran, they could build missile technology that could destroy the United States. So that's why you're not allowed to hire people who are not permanent residents. Logical.
turns.zh: 是的。拜登政府还在起诉SpaceX。他们因为SpaceX没有雇佣寻求庇护者而提起大规模诉讼。所以有人说‘哦，Elon在编造，这届政府并没有针对SpaceX’。我说，兄弟，司法部对SpaceX提起了大规模诉讼，因为我们没有雇佣寻求庇护者，尽管雇佣非永久居民是非法的。所以这里存在矛盾：一方面有法律要求你必须雇佣寻求庇护者，另一方面又有法律规定火箭公司（属于先进武器技术）的员工必须是永久居民。而寻求庇护者不是永久居民。所以雇佣寻求庇护者既合法又非法。那么拜登政府为什么要提起大规模诉讼？这是公开信息，不是我的想象。他们为什么对SpaceX提起如此大规模的诉讼？他们极其敌对。这根本说不通，哪怕第一天有人看一眼就该驳回。如果它既非法你又试图执行，你无法执行。是的，这是一家先进武器公司。这太疯狂了。应该被驳回。实际上有《国际武器贸易条例》，这是一项法律，确保只有美国永久居民才能在先进武器公司工作。火箭是先进武器。核武器、生物武器等也是如此。因为如果有人为SpaceX工作后去了朝鲜或伊朗，他们可以建造导弹技术摧毁美国。所以这就是为什么你不能雇佣非永久居民。合乎逻辑。

turns.en: So is that lawsuit still pending?
turns.zh: 那个诉讼还在进行吗？

turns.en: It was just dismissed.
turns.zh: 刚刚被驳回了。

turns.en: How long was it going on for?
turns.zh: 持续了多久？

turns.en: A couple years.
turns.zh: 几年。

turns.en: Holy yeah. That's the other thing that drives me crazy. People don't understand that if you sanction lawfare like that, if you sanction attacking your political enemies, someone's going to do that to you. If the wrong people get in office, if new people get in office four years from now, eight years from now, who knows who it's going to be. You've already set a precedent. You've already attacked someone, charged them with 34 felonies where they're really just misdemeanors and they've passed the statute of limitations. And now you're talking all over the news that this is a convicted felon. They kept saying 'convicted felon, convicted felon,' and everybody knows what it is. Yes, it's terrifying. It's terrifying they could do it so brazenly to a guy who was the president for four years.
turns.zh: 天哪。这让我抓狂。人们不明白，如果你纵容这种法律战，如果你纵容攻击政治敌人，总有一天别人也会这样对你。如果错误的人上台，四年后、八年后新的人上台，谁知道会是谁。你已经开了先例。你已经攻击了一个人，指控他34项重罪，而实际上只是轻罪，而且已经过了诉讼时效。现在你在新闻上到处说他是‘被定罪的重罪犯’。他们不停地说‘被定罪的重罪犯，被定罪的重罪犯’，每个人都知道是怎么回事。是的，太可怕了。他们能如此厚颜无耻地对待一个当了四年总统的人，太可怕了。

turns.en: That lawsuit was funded by Reid Hoffman, who is a major Democratic donor and also an FD client. The plot thickens.
turns.zh: 那个诉讼是由Reid Hoffman资助的，他是主要的民主党捐赠者，也是FD客户。情节变得复杂了。

turns.en: The plot thickens. Jesus Christ. Yes, it's just so blatant, it's so obvious. The SpaceX lawsuit, the Trump stuff, it's just so obvious. Yes, known FD clients who are obviously extremely powerful, politically and very wealthy. Bill Clinton, Reid Hoffman, and some others, but those three. So why was Reid Hoffman so intent on destroying Trump? Do you think it's because they're worried about the list coming out?
turns.zh: 情节变得复杂了。天哪。是的，太明目张胆了，太明显了。SpaceX的诉讼，特朗普的事，都太明显了。是的，已知的FD客户，显然极其强大，政治上和财富上都如此。比尔·克林顿、Reid Hoffman，还有其他几个，但主要是这三个。那么为什么Reid Hoffman如此执着于摧毁特朗普？你觉得是因为他们担心名单曝光吗？

turns.en: Yeah, one of the reasons. So it's so frustrating to be sitting in the situation where the list isn't coming out.
turns.zh: 是的，原因之一。所以身处名单不公开的处境非常令人沮丧。

turns.en: Well, it better come out. I mean, hopefully tomorrow. Why would they release today? I don't know. What's the point in giving these people a happy folder to wave around in front of the camera with nothing in it that's new? Doesn't make any sense. It's not encouraging. Like I said, the tough thing that they've got is they've been made captain of a ship with a hostile crew. So they don't have magical powers. You get made captain of a ship with a hostile crew, you still have a hostile crew. You've got to bring in people who are going to be helpful as opposed to obstructionist. But I think the public will be right frustrated if no one is prosecuted for the FD client list. No one at all. At least the top five or something. There should at least be an attempted prosecution of the worst offenders. Particularly if Ghislaine Maxwell is in jail for sex trafficking. Yes, because sex trafficking occurred. She's in jail for it. So who are the clients?
turns.zh: 嗯，最好公开。我是说，希望明天。他们为什么今天发布？我不知道。给这些人一个空文件夹在镜头前挥舞有什么意义？一点新内容都没有。这说不通。不鼓舞人心。就像我说的，他们面临的困境是，他们成了一艘充满敌意船员的船的船长。他们没有魔法。你成了一艘充满敌意船员的船的船长，你仍然有敌意的船员。你必须引进有帮助的人，而不是阻挠者。但我认为，如果没有人因FD客户名单被起诉，公众会非常失望。一个都没有。至少前五名之类的。至少应该尝试起诉最严重的违法者。特别是如果Ghislaine Maxwell因性贩卖入狱。是的，因为性贩卖发生了。她因此入狱。那么客户是谁？

turns.en: Yeah, how do you...
turns.zh: 是的，你怎么……

turns.en: Put someone in jail and you don't even name the clients? That sounds kind of insane.
turns.zh: 把人关进监狱，你连客户名字都不提？这听起来有点离谱。

turns.en: Yes, it would. Because it's stunning that they've been able to hold it back for so long. It's really kind of amazing. When people say that people can't keep secrets, what are you talking about? Look at this. A bunch of these things are common knowledge, but we don't actually have the proof. The proof is there. There are lots of videos. It's like a mountain of evidence. When they raided Epstein's place, there would have been a mountain of evidence. Where is that mountain? What did you do with it? Who took possession of the evidence? Specifically, the individuals. Where are the tapes? How many levels of clearance do I have to get to get into the vault?
turns.zh: 是的，会。因为他们能隐瞒这么久，真是令人震惊。这真的很了不起。当人们说没人能保守秘密时，你在说什么？看看这个。很多事情都是常识，但我们实际上没有证据。证据就在那里。有很多视频。就像一座证据山。当他们突袭爱泼斯坦的住所时，应该有一座证据山。那座山在哪里？你们怎么处理了？谁拿走了证据？具体来说，那些个人。录像带在哪里？我需要多少级权限才能进入那个保险库？

turns.en: We need people who are really good with computers and technology. I remember seeing this photo when they raided his home on the island. They got everything. There must have been so much stuff on that island. If it wasn't there, where was it? It has to be uploaded somewhere. There has to be some chain of evidence or chain of custody. There has to be a mountain of evidence.
turns.zh: 我们需要非常擅长计算机和技术的人。我记得看到过这张照片，他们突袭了他在岛上的家。他们拿走了所有东西。那个岛上肯定有很多东西。如果不在那里，那在哪里？它一定被上传到了某个地方。必须有一条证据链或监管链。肯定有一座证据山。

turns.en: The other thing they're going to talk about is UAPs. They're going to release all the UAP information. So you're the guy to ask about this. What possibility is there that there is some sort of advanced propulsion system technology being worked on in secret, and that they're trying to cover this up with talk of aliens and alien tech? Is it possible that there's some very secret program in cahoots with defense contractors developing advanced propulsion systems for these drones?
turns.zh: 他们要谈的另一件事是UAP。他们会公布所有UAP信息。所以你是问这个问题的合适人选。有没有可能某种先进的推进系统技术正在秘密研发，而他们试图用外星人和外星技术的说法来掩盖？有没有可能有一个非常秘密的项目，与国防承包商勾结，为这些无人机开发先进的推进系统？

turns.en: SpaceX has the most advanced rocket technology in the world. I think I'd know. To the best of my knowledge, there is not any magic there. There's not some super advanced propulsion technology. There have been people who theorized different gravity drives, but nothing has ever gotten past the theoretical stage. Nothing even works in theory. I would like this to exist, to be clear. I have top secret clearance, equivalent to an all-access pass. I don't think they're hiding it from me. I don't think they could, unless it's completely within these weapons manufacturing corporations. I know these companies like Boeing, Lockheed, Northrop. They do some interesting things, but they do not have a breakthrough. I'm confident they do not have a breakthrough.
turns.zh: SpaceX拥有世界上最先进的火箭技术。我想我知道。据我所知，那里没有什么魔法。没有什么超级先进的推进技术。有人提出过不同的重力驱动理论，但从未超出理论阶段。甚至理论上都不成立。我想说清楚，我希望这种东西存在。我有最高机密权限，相当于全能通行证。我不认为他们对我隐瞒。我不认为他们能隐瞒，除非它完全在这些武器制造公司内部。我了解这些公司，比如波音、洛克希德、诺斯罗普。他们做了一些有趣的事情，但他们没有突破。我确信他们没有突破。

turns.en: When you hear people like, why not compete with SpaceX and make a better rocket? They could make a lot of money beating SpaceX with better rockets. My thought was, what if it's just a drone and you can't have a biological entity inside because it bursts from the speed? A human couldn't tolerate the force, so they're just drones.
turns.zh: 当你听到有人说，为什么不和SpaceX竞争，造出更好的火箭？他们可以用更好的火箭击败SpaceX赚很多钱。我的想法是，如果它只是一个无人机，里面不能有生物实体，因为速度会导致它爆裂？人类无法承受那种力量，所以它们只是无人机。

turns.en: I don't think so.
turns.zh: 我不这么认为。

turns.en: What do you think people like Ryan Graves and Commander David Fravor are seeing? I want cool things to exist. Do I want UFOs to exist? Yes, because that would be really interesting. It's a more boring world where UFOs don't exist. I'd like it to exist. I hope we find something. But I have not seen anything. SpaceX launches 90% of all satellite mass to orbit. China does about 5%, and the rest of the world, including Boeing, Lockheed, and everyone, does 5%. Why wouldn't they use this to defeat SpaceX?
turns.zh: 你觉得像Ryan Graves和David Fravor指挥官这样的人看到了什么？我希望酷的东西存在。我希望UFO存在吗？是的，因为那会非常有趣。没有UFO的世界更无聊。我希望它存在。我希望我们能找到什么。但我什么都没看到。SpaceX发射了90%的卫星质量到轨道。中国约占5%，世界其他地区，包括波音、洛克希德等，占5%。他们为什么不用这个来击败SpaceX？

turns.en: That's why I asked you. It would make sense.
turns.zh: 这就是我问你的原因。这说得通。

turns.en: What do you think these people are seeing? When you have reliable people like Commander David Fravor, who had that infamous Tic Tac experience off the coast of San Diego, they got this thing on video. They tracked it going from 50,000 feet above sea level to 50 feet in like a second. They also have video evidence of this thing accelerating at great speed, eyewitness accounts from two different jets. Does anybody have a high-res video or photo of this thing? There's a video where they're locked onto it and it shoots off frame. The systems they used on fighter jets in 2004 were potentially like Windows 95. Someone did a curve of the resolution of UFOs and cameras. UFO resolution has stayed flat despite megapixels going super high. According to Christopher Mellon, who worked in the State Department, he said they have high-resolution photos and videos of these things, but it's all locked away. Whenever people say that to me, don't even tell me that. Just leak it, for God's sake. Put it out there. Let it slip.
turns.zh: 你觉得这些人看到了什么？当你有像David Fravor指挥官这样可靠的人，他在圣地亚哥海岸外有过那次臭名昭著的Tic Tac经历，他们用视频拍到了这个东西。他们追踪到它从海平面以上5万英尺在一秒钟内下降到50英尺。他们还有视频证据显示这个东西以极快速度加速，以及两架不同喷气式飞机的目击者证词。有人有这个东西的高清视频或照片吗？有一个视频，他们锁定了它，然后它飞出了画面。2004年战斗机使用的系统可能像Windows 95一样。有人画了一条UFO分辨率和相机分辨率的曲线。尽管像素变得超高，UFO分辨率却保持不变。据在国务院工作过的Christopher Mellon说，他说他们有这些东西的高清照片和视频，但都被锁起来了。每当有人这样对我说，别告诉我。看在上帝的份上，泄露出来吧。把它放出来。让它泄露出去。

turns.en: There are a couple photos, but they're grainy. There's not one thing that I've ever looked at and gone, 'Holy, that's it. That's what I've been looking for.' You could ask our Grok AI right now to create a high-res image of an alien spacecraft over Austin, and it's going to do a great job. So why don't we have at least that?
turns.zh: 有几张照片，但很模糊。我从来没有看过任何东西然后说，‘天哪，就是这个。这就是我一直在找的。’你现在可以问我们的Grok AI，让它生成一张奥斯汀上空外星飞船的高清图像，它会做得很好。那我们为什么至少没有那个？

turns.en: I want to believe. That's the problem. My brain starts going, 'Oh, come on, this is no fun.' I want it to be real. I want there to be at least some advanced propulsion system. If not, what are all these people seeing? If we're not being occasionally visited by things that are smart enough to hide, we might be. These aliens are very subtle.
turns.zh: 我想相信。这就是问题所在。我的大脑开始想，‘哦，拜托，这不好玩。’我希望它是真的。我希望至少有一些先进的推进系统。如果不是，那这些人都在看什么？如果我们没有被足够聪明能隐藏的东西偶尔访问，那可能我们就是。这些外星人非常微妙。

turns.en: You keep saying that. It's a good line. It's a solid line because it's pretty accurate. I just want to see some high-res video of aliens. How are they evading all the cameras? The ones you do get are just some faraway light moving weirdly, and it could be a lot of things.
turns.zh: 你一直这么说。这是个好说法。这是个扎实的说法，因为它相当准确。我只是想看一些外星人的高清视频。他们是怎么避开所有摄像头的？你拍到的那些只是远处奇怪移动的光，可能有很多种可能。

turns.en: Want to believe? Yeah, I mean, there have been multiple times where the Air Force and Navy have called SpaceX and said they think they've seen aliens, and we're like, 'Was it at this time, in this state, in this location?' They're like, 'Yes.' 'How do you know that's us?' There's been a lot of that. Those are our satellites. Those are our satellites. They're like, 'No, they're not.' I'm like, 'Yeah, yeah, they're definitely our satellites.' Oh yeah, people see the SpaceX satellites all the time whizzing by. Yeah, they're our satellites, and they are moving at 16,000 miles per hour, so it's pretty fast. And there's also stuff that the United States government does have that gets mistaken for UFOs. I remember the first time I saw a stealth bomber. We were filming Fear Factor, it was right after 2003, right after the war had broken out, and they were flying a stealth bomber down in Palmdale. I was like, 'Holy... if I didn't know what that was, I would 100% think that's from another world.' When you see it, cool, cool, yeah, really cool. I mean, it doesn't look like a human thing. It looks like something from Battlestar Galactica. It does look awesome. I mean, they're not stealthy against any advanced radar system, by the way. It doesn't work anymore. It doesn't work. No, it's old school stuff. They're only stealthy against old radars. Oh, okay. I mean, you can still see them. They're not invisible, right? Right, it's not like a cloaking device from Star Trek.
turns.zh: 愿意相信吗？是的，我的意思是，有好几次空军和海军给SpaceX打电话，说他们觉得看到了外星人，我们就问：‘是不是在这个时间、这个州、这个地点？’他们说：‘是的。’‘你怎么知道那是我们？’这种情况发生过很多次。那是我们的卫星。那是我们的卫星。他们说：‘不，不是你们的。’我说：‘对对对，绝对是我们的卫星。’哦，是的，人们经常看到SpaceX的卫星飞过。是的，那是我们的卫星，它们以每小时16000英里的速度移动，所以非常快。而且美国政府也有一些东西被误认为是UFO。我记得第一次看到隐形轰炸机的时候。我们在拍《恐惧因素》，那是2003年之后，战争刚爆发不久，他们在帕姆代尔飞了一架隐形轰炸机。我当时想：‘天哪，如果我不知道那是什么，我百分之百会认为那是来自另一个世界的东西。’当你看到它时，很酷，很酷，是的，真的很酷。我的意思是，它看起来不像人类的东西。它看起来像《太空堡垒卡拉狄加》里的东西。它确实看起来很厉害。我的意思是，顺便说一句，它们对任何先进的雷达系统都不是隐形的。它不再起作用了。它不起作用了。不，那是老式的东西。它们只对老式雷达隐形。哦，好吧。我的意思是，你仍然可以看到它们。它们不是隐形的，对吧？对，它不像《星际迷航》里的隐形装置。

turns.en: Did you see when me and Lex watched the rocket get caught live while it was happening? That to me was one of the... to see it actually... I've seen videos of it happen, but to see it actually live was one of the coolest things. You're like, 'Wow, we are in the future.' Right? I mean, nobody else can do that. Yeah, it's true. Nobody else can do that. That's fact. Yeah, it's pretty wild. It's 'cause I'm an alien. It's time you... I'm an alien, and I keep trying to tell people I'm an alien, but they don't believe me.
turns.zh: 你看到我和Lex一起看火箭被捕获的直播了吗？那对我来说是……真正看到它……我看过它发生的视频，但真正看到直播是最酷的事情之一。你会觉得：‘哇，我们活在未来。’对吧？我的意思是，没有其他人能做到这一点。是的，这是真的。没有其他人能做到这一点。这是事实。是的，这太疯狂了。因为我是外星人。是时候了……我是外星人，我一直试图告诉人们我是外星人，但他们不相信我。

turns.en: I believe you. Okay, thank you. I believe you. That's been my suspicion all along, that you're a friendly alien. Like, there's nothing wrong with aliens. I like people from everywhere, yeah, even other planets.
turns.zh: 我相信你。好的，谢谢。我相信你。我一直怀疑你是个友好的外星人。就像，外星人没什么不好。我喜欢来自任何地方的人，是的，甚至其他星球。

ts.sec: Next steps in rocket technology: full and rapid reusability

turns.en: What's next? Now that you can catch rockets, what's the ultimate expression of rocket technology? What comes after this?
turns.zh: 接下来是什么？既然你能抓住火箭，火箭技术的终极体现是什么？这之后会是什么？

turns.en: Well, the fundamental breakthrough we're aiming for at SpaceX is a fully and rapidly reusable orbital rocket where both stages are fully and rapidly reusable. With our Falcon rocket, we are able to reuse the main stage and the nose cone, but we're not able to reuse the upper stage. And it still takes us at least a few days from when the main stage lands to when we can fly again. So it's not fully reusable because we lose the upper stage, which costs $10 million to build. And the main stage is not as reusable as an aircraft; you can't just refuel it and fly it. It requires work for a couple of days. But the Starship design is the first design that is capable of full and rapid reusability, where that is one of the possible outcomes. Once you have full and rapid reusability, the cost of access to space drops by a factor of 100. It's like 100 times cheaper. By some metrics, it's a thousand times cheaper. And when you factor in orbital refilling, so you refill on orbit, it can drop the cost per ton to the surface of Mars by a factor of 10,000.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们在SpaceX追求的根本性突破是一种完全且快速可重复使用的轨道火箭，其中两级都完全且快速可重复使用。使用我们的猎鹰火箭，我们能够重复使用主级和鼻锥，但无法重复使用上级。而且从主级着陆到再次飞行，至少需要几天时间。所以它并不是完全可重复使用的，因为我们失去了上级，而上级的建造成本为1000万美元。而且主级的可重复使用性不如飞机；你不能简单地给它加油然后飞行。它需要几天的维护工作。但星舰设计是第一个能够实现完全且快速可重复使用的设计，这是可能的结果之一。一旦你实现了完全且快速的可重复使用，进入太空的成本将下降100倍。也就是便宜100倍。根据某些指标，甚至便宜1000倍。而且当你考虑到轨道加注，即在轨道上加油时，每吨货物运送到火星表面的成本可以下降10000倍。

turns.en: Whoa, yeah. So what has to improve in order to make it reusable?
turns.zh: 哇，是的。那么要实现可重复使用，需要改进什么？

turns.en: Well, we're pretty close to being able to rapidly reuse the booster for Starship. That's why it comes back and gets caught by the arms, and then the arms place it back on the launch mount. Now, we still have a little bit of engine damage, a little bit of heat shield damage. There are tweaks that are needed, but we're pretty close to achieving full and rapid reusability of the booster. The ship, I think we'll achieve reusability of the ship this year, and I think we'll achieve rapid reusability of the whole stack, ship and booster, next year. This is the fundamental breakthrough required for life to become multiplanetary.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们已经非常接近能够快速重复使用星舰的助推器了。这就是为什么它回来并被机械臂抓住，然后机械臂把它放回发射台。现在，我们仍然有一些发动机损坏，一些隔热罩损坏。需要做一些调整，但我们非常接近实现助推器的完全且快速可重复使用。至于飞船，我认为我们今年将实现飞船的可重复使用，并且我认为明年我们将实现整个堆栈（飞船和助推器）的快速可重复使用。这是生命成为多行星物种所需的根本性突破。

turns.en: What needs to improve in order to make it reusable? What's wrong with it right now?
turns.zh: 要实现可重复使用，需要改进什么？现在有什么问题？

turns.en: On the ship side, the toughest problem is the heat shield. No one has ever developed a fully reusable orbital heat shield. Because when you come in from orbital velocity, you come in like a flaming meteor. You're just a raging ball of fire. And it's hard to have a heat shield that doesn't partially melt or get destroyed in that process. That wasn't a problem we were able to solve with Falcon 9; that's why the upper stage burns up on re-entry. With Starship, the ship portion, we have to solve making a fully reusable orbital heat shield, a problem that has never been solved before. For a while, I was like, 'I'm not sure this is solvable.' At this point, I think it is solvable. It requires detailed iteration on the heat shield tiles. We've vertically integrated the manufacturing of the heat shield tiles because there was no supplier that could provide us with the materials that were needed. You need to make essentially this very fine verelli of glass and aluminum oxide fibers. Aluminum oxide is basically sapphire. So it's like glass and sapphire, very fine fibers in exactly the right geometry, with special coatings, in order to have the heat shield tile be reusable, not melt, but not be so brittle that it gets damaged on ascent or descent. It's kind of like the brittleness of a coffee cup. And the rocket is shaking like hell. You saw it firsthand. Imagine you're at ground zero of that rocket; you feel how much shaking it was when you're 5 miles away. Imagine if you're right there. So you're shaking these things that are as brittle as a coffee cup, trying not to have them crack or break, and then not have them melt. You have several thousand of these things. If even a few of them break, it's not reusable.
turns.zh: 在飞船方面，最棘手的问题是隔热罩。从来没有人开发过完全可重复使用的轨道隔热罩。因为当你从轨道速度返回时，你会像一颗燃烧的流星一样进入。你就是一个狂暴的火球。很难有一个隔热罩不会在这个过程中部分熔化或损坏。这不是我们在猎鹰9号上能够解决的问题；这就是为什么上级在再入时会烧毁。对于星舰，飞船部分，我们必须解决制造完全可重复使用的轨道隔热罩的问题，这是一个从未被解决过的问题。有一段时间，我想：‘我不确定这是否可以解决。’在这一点上，我认为它是可以解决的。它需要对隔热罩瓦片进行详细的迭代。我们已经垂直整合了隔热罩瓦片的制造，因为没有供应商能够提供所需的材料。你需要制造这种非常精细的玻璃和氧化铝纤维的编织物。氧化铝基本上就是蓝宝石。所以它就像玻璃和蓝宝石，非常精细的纤维，具有完全正确的几何形状，并带有特殊涂层，以便隔热罩瓦片可重复使用，不会熔化，但又不会太脆以至于在上升或下降过程中损坏。它有点像咖啡杯的脆性。而火箭在剧烈震动。你亲眼看到了。想象一下你在火箭的震中；当你在5英里外时，你都能感觉到它有多大的震动。想象一下如果你就在那里。所以你在震动这些像咖啡杯一样脆的东西，试图不让它们破裂或断裂，同时又不让它们熔化。你有几千个这样的东西。即使其中几个破裂，它就不能重复使用了。

turns.en: Is there innovation being done in the materials technology at SpaceX where you're constantly trying to find and tweak a better version of this?
turns.zh: SpaceX在材料技术方面是否有创新，你们是否在不断地寻找和调整更好的版本？

turns.en: Yes, it's a very difficult problem. No one has ever solved it.
turns.zh: 是的，这是一个非常困难的问题。从来没有人解决过它。

turns.en: You've got to get the exact right materials combination, the right molecules in the right shape, and then apply that heat shield perfectly to the rocket with no mistakes. There's a reason that no one solved this before; it's a very difficult problem. So we had to vertically integrate the entire manufacturing of the tile from basic raw materials to a finished tile, rebuild the entire supply chain from basic raw materials. So you're just inputting silicon and aluminum oxides.
turns.zh: 你必须找到精确的材料组合，正确的分子形状，然后完美地将隔热罩应用到火箭上，不能有任何差错。这个问题以前没人解决是有原因的，它非常困难。所以我们不得不垂直整合从基础原材料到成品隔热瓦的整个制造过程，重建从基础原材料开始的整个供应链。你只需要输入硅和铝的氧化物。

turns.en: What is the difference between the way you guys do it versus the way they used to do it for the space shuttle?
turns.zh: 你们的方法和航天飞机过去的方法有什么不同？

turns.en: Well, the space shuttle used quite dense carbon-carbon tiles. They were thick and heavy, but also subject to cracking. What happened is the foam broke off, hit the tile, cracked the tile. Then on entry, the cracked or broken tiles weren't able to shield the shuttle, so the plasma got in, melted the primary structure, and the whole shuttle broke apart. So you can't have something that's as brittle as the space shuttle tiles.
turns.zh: 嗯，航天飞机使用了相当致密的碳-碳隔热瓦。它们又厚又重，而且容易开裂。发生的事情是泡沫脱落，击中隔热瓦，导致隔热瓦开裂。然后在再入时，开裂或破损的隔热瓦无法保护航天飞机，等离子体进入，融化了主结构，整个航天飞机解体了。所以你不能有像航天飞机隔热瓦那样脆的东西。

turns.en: There's footage of that, right?
turns.zh: 有那段录像，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah, and debris over the whole United States. They got almost all the pieces. The full technical explanation would be understood by about six people listening to this. There was a lot of brilliant engineering in the space shuttle tiles, and a bunch of the heat shielding wasn't even tiles; it was actually silica blankets, like felt blankets essentially. If you look closely, you'll see they're actually heat blankets, not tiles, in some areas. But they would have cracked tiles, and occasionally tiles would fall off. There were a few close calls where tiles fell off but weren't in a super vulnerable position. So it would take them several months, like eight or nine months, to refurbish a space shuttle between each flight. So it wasn't really reusable, and certainly wasn't rapid.
turns.zh: 是的，碎片遍布全美国。他们几乎找回了所有碎片。完整的技术解释大概只有六个人能听懂。航天飞机隔热瓦有很多出色的工程设计，而且很多隔热材料甚至不是瓦片，实际上是二氧化硅毯子，基本上就像毛毡毯子。如果你仔细看，会发现有些地方其实是隔热毯，而不是瓦片。但他们的瓦片会开裂，偶尔还会脱落。有过几次险情，瓦片脱落了，但不在特别脆弱的位置。所以每次飞行之间，他们需要花好几个月，比如八九个月，来翻新航天飞机。所以它并不是真正可重复使用的，当然也不快速。

turns.en: So it's a very hard problem.
turns.zh: 所以这是一个非常困难的问题。

turns.en: Yes. You've also got to attach the tiles in a way that enables the structure underneath to move, to expand and contract, even though you've got these very rigid tiles. The main tanks, which take on cryogenic propellant, will contract when you put in the cryogenic propellant, but then when you come in and get very hot, they will expand. So you're contracting and expanding the gap between these rigid tiles. How much? It varies depending on where you are on the vehicle. If you're in the cryogenic tank section, you can see a 10-20% difference in the gap. It's pretty significant. You can't just jam the tiles together; if you butted them up, they would all crack because there's too much movement. There's also some amount of body bending. As the ship is ascending, when the engines steer, there's a little bit of movement. So if the tiles are too close together, they'll crack and snap. You have to have a gap, like a plane wing will move, a plane body will move too. But if you have too much of a gap, then the heat gets past the tile and melts the structure.
turns.zh: 是的。你还必须以某种方式固定隔热瓦，让下面的结构能够移动、膨胀和收缩，尽管这些隔热瓦非常坚硬。主燃料箱装有低温推进剂，注入低温推进剂时会收缩，但再入时变得非常热，又会膨胀。所以你在收缩和膨胀这些刚性隔热瓦之间的间隙。间隙变化多大？取决于你在飞行器的哪个位置。如果在低温燃料箱段，间隙变化可达10-20%。这相当显著。你不能简单地把隔热瓦挤在一起；如果它们紧挨着，就会因为移动太大而全部开裂。还有一定程度的机身弯曲。当飞船上升时，发动机转向会产生一点移动。所以如果隔热瓦靠得太近，它们就会开裂和断裂。你必须留出间隙，就像机翼会移动，机身也会移动一样。但如果间隙太大，热量就会穿过隔热瓦，熔化结构。

turns.en: How large are these tiles?
turns.zh: 这些隔热瓦有多大？

turns.en: They're about that big. They're not all exactly the same size, but they're sort of hexagonal tiles. You can't 3D print the whole thing; you can't have one structure; it has to be tiles because it has to have that ability to move. There's no 3D printer that big, and you wouldn't want to anyway; you have to have something that can flex. So you have expansion and contraction. You're putting in liquid oxygen, which is about -300 degrees Fahrenheit, actually we subcool it to -330 degrees Fahrenheit, so it's very cold. And then it will be several hundred degrees, maybe a thousand degrees Fahrenheit, potentially on re-entry. So you have this huge temperature swing. The thermal expansion is substantial, combined with body bending. You have to take the worst-case body bending and thermal expansion/contraction. This was a very hard problem.
turns.zh: 它们大约这么大。并不完全一样大，但大致是六边形。你不能3D打印整个东西；不能只有一个结构；必须是瓦片，因为它需要能够移动。没有那么大尺寸的3D打印机，而且你也不想要；你需要能够弯曲的东西。所以有膨胀和收缩。你注入液氧，温度大约零下300华氏度，实际上我们过冷到零下330华氏度，所以非常冷。然后再入时可能达到几百甚至一千华氏度。所以温度变化巨大。热膨胀很大，再加上机身弯曲。你必须考虑最坏情况的机身弯曲和热膨胀/收缩。这是一个非常困难的问题。

turns.en: Delicate balance. But you're confident that you guys are going to be able to crack it at this point?
turns.zh: 微妙的平衡。但你现在有信心你们能解决这个问题吗？

turns.en: I'm confident that it is solvable. It just needs a certain number of versions. That's why when these things blow up, you're like, yeah, we expect them to blow up. What would be really helpful is for us to get the ship back so we can study where we had cracked tiles or lost tiles. Why did we have a cracked tile? Was it because the gap was too big or too small? Maybe there was a height difference between the tiles. Maybe we need to change the chemical composition. If we can get the damn ship back intact, we can iterate a lot better. We'll get it back intact. I think we'll get it back intact this year. We'll probably recover the ship sometime this year, and then we might be able to refly one with a fair bit of work by the end of this year. But it's going to take us many iterations before we can achieve rapid reusability, where the ship comes back, lands, gets caught like the booster with the arms, and then the arms place it on top of the booster and it launches again. That will reduce the cost of access to space by a factor of 100.
turns.zh: 我相信这是可以解决的。只是需要一定数量的版本迭代。这就是为什么当这些东西爆炸时，你会说，是的，我们预料到它们会爆炸。真正有帮助的是让飞船返回，这样我们就可以研究哪里出现了开裂或脱落的隔热瓦。为什么会有开裂的隔热瓦？是因为间隙太大还是太小？也许隔热瓦之间有高度差。也许我们需要改变化学成分。如果我们能让该死的飞船完好无损地返回，我们就能更好地迭代。我们会让它完好无损地返回。我认为今年就能做到。我们可能会在今年某个时候回收飞船，然后经过相当多的工作，可能在今年年底前再次飞行。但我们需要很多次迭代才能实现快速可重复使用，即飞船返回、着陆、像助推器一样被机械臂抓住，然后机械臂把它放在助推器顶部，再次发射。这将把进入太空的成本降低100倍。

turns.en: What is the process of returning these people that are stuck in the space station?
turns.zh: 让那些被困在空间站的人返回的过程是怎样的？

turns.en: We send SpaceX Dragon to the space station all the time. We've now taken people to orbit and back, over 50 astronauts. So it's just a matter of doing it.
turns.zh: 我们一直向空间站发送SpaceX的龙飞船。我们已经将超过50名宇航员送入轨道并返回。所以这只是执行的问题。

turns.en: Is it a matter of waiting? We do it routinely basically?
turns.zh: 只是等待的问题吗？基本上我们已经常规化操作了？

turns.en: We've been doing this for a few years.
turns.zh: 我们已经这样做了几年了。

turns.en: When is this rescue mission going to launch?
turns.zh: 这次救援任务什么时候发射？

turns.en: Probably about four weeks or so, depending on weather and other factors.
turns.zh: 大概四周左右，取决于天气和其他因素。

turns.en: Let's take it past the point where you have these scales, you have a reusable ship, and you've got it dialed in. Then what are the steps? What's the next step after that? Is it an unmanned voyage to Mars?
turns.zh: 我们跳过你已经有了这些规模、可重复使用的飞船并且一切就绪的阶段。接下来是什么步骤？在那之后下一步是什么？是无人前往火星吗？

turns.en: First unmanned fly to Mars. Earth and Mars orbit synchronize every two years, or every 26 months technically. The next orbital synchronization is November of next year, and you can launch plus minus a month roughly. So the default plan is to launch hopefully several Starships to Mars at the end of next year.
turns.zh: 首先无人飞往火星。地球和火星的轨道每两年同步一次，严格来说是每26个月。下一次轨道同步是明年11月，发射窗口大约前后一个月。所以默认计划是希望在明年底发射多艘星舰前往火星。

turns.en: What would they be doing?
turns.zh: 它们会做什么？

turns.en: At first we're just going to try to land on Mars and see if we succeed in landing. If we were able to send five ships, do all five land intact, or do we add some craters to Mars? If we add some craters, we've got to be a bit more cautious about sending people. We need to make sure the thing lands safely.
turns.zh: 一开始我们只是尝试在火星着陆，看看能否成功着陆。如果我们能发送五艘飞船，是全部完好着陆，还是给火星添几个坑？如果添了坑，那送人就得更加谨慎。我们必须确保它安全着陆。

turns.en: How does it land on Mars?
turns.zh: 它如何在火星着陆？

turns.en: With rockets. It'll just land on legs. It will be autonomous, completely autonomous. Mars is too far to remote control from Earth because of speed of light constraints. At closest approach, Mars is roughly four light minutes away; when it's on the other side of the sun, it's about 12 light minutes. Round trip would be like 40 minutes best case.
turns.zh: 用火箭着陆。它会用着陆腿着陆。完全是自主的。火星太远，无法从地球遥控，因为光速限制。最近时火星大约4光分远；在太阳另一侧时大约12光分。往返最快也要40分钟。

turns.en: Once you do that, how long before you start sending people?
turns.zh: 一旦做到这一点，多久之后开始送人？

turns.en: We're going to try to go as fast as possible. You can think of this as a race against time: can we make Mars self-sufficient before civilization has some sort of future fork in the road, like a nuclear war, or we get hit by a meteor, or simply civilization might die with a whimper in adult diapers instead of with a bang. I think we can do this in maybe 15 Earth-Mars synchronization events, so basically like 30 years, if we have an exponential increase every two years in the number of people and tonnage to Mars. As a rough approximation, we need about a million tons to the surface of Mars and maybe a million people to actually have a civilization.
turns.zh: 我们会尽可能快地推进。你可以把这看作一场与时间的赛跑：我们能否在文明遭遇某种未来分岔路——比如核战争、被小行星撞击，或者干脆文明在成人尿布中呜咽消亡而非轰然崩塌——之前，让火星实现自给自足？我认为我们可以在大约15个地球-火星同步周期内做到，也就是大约30年，前提是每两年送往火星的人数和吨位呈指数增长。粗略估算，我们需要将大约一百万吨物资和一百万人送到火星表面，才能真正建立一个文明。

turns.en: Would you terraform?
turns.zh: 你会进行地球化改造吗？

turns.en: Eventually terraform, but at first people would live in protected environments like domes and underground. Terraforming would take too long. We are at a point in time where, for the first time in the 4.5 billion year history of Earth, it is possible to extend consciousness beyond our home planet. That window may be open for a long time or a short time. I hope it's long, but it might only be short. We should make sure we extend the light of consciousness to Mars before civilization either extinguishes or subsides. All that needs to happen is that the technology level of Earth drops below what is necessary to send spaceships to Mars. If there's some really destructive war, a natural cataclysm, or simply the birth rate is so low that we die in adult diapers with a whimper—that's one of the most fossil outcomes for a lot of countries. Japan and Korea are headed that way. At current birth rates, in three generations Korea will be about 4% of its current size. That's insane. Basically, population collapse happens fast and seems to be accelerating in most parts of the world. So from my standpoint, this is the first time it's been possible to extend life and consciousness beyond Earth. Maybe that window will be open for a long time, but it might only be open for a short time. We should make sure we make life multiplanetary and consciousness multiplanetary while it's possible.
turns.zh: 最终会进行地球化改造，但一开始人们会住在穹顶和地下等受保护的环境中。地球化改造耗时太长。我们正处于地球45亿年历史中首次有可能将意识扩展到地球之外的时代。这个窗口可能开放很久，也可能很短。我希望它很长，但也可能很短。我们应该确保在文明熄灭或衰退之前，将意识之光延伸到火星。唯一需要发生的是，地球的技术水平下降到不足以向火星发射飞船。如果发生某种毁灭性战争、自然灾难，或者仅仅因为出生率太低，我们在成人尿布中呜咽消亡——这是许多国家最糟糕的结局之一。日本和韩国正朝这个方向发展。按照目前的出生率，三代之后韩国人口将只剩下现在的4%左右。这太疯狂了。基本上，人口崩溃发生得很快，而且在世界大部分地区似乎还在加速。所以在我看来，这是第一次有可能将生命和意识扩展到地球之外。也许这个窗口会开放很久，但也可能只开放很短时间。我们应该趁可能的时候，确保生命和意识成为多行星的。

turns.en: That's the goal of SpaceX. It's a smart goal if you take into consideration how vulnerable this planet really is. There's always some new story about something that might come and hit us 30 years from now with a 3% chance, and we really can't stop that right now. We don't really have the technology to even know how many rocks are coming our way. There's stuff that comes behind the Sun that we can't see until it's pretty close.
turns.zh: 这就是SpaceX的目标。考虑到这个星球有多脆弱，这是一个明智的目标。总有一些新闻说某样东西30年后可能有3%的概率撞击我们，而我们现在根本无法阻止。我们甚至没有技术知道有多少岩石正朝我们飞来。有些东西从太阳后面过来，直到很近我们才能看到。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: What about the journey to Mars? It's a long journey, six months roughly. What about micrometeors or other possibilities? What can you do to mitigate that?
turns.zh: 那去火星的旅程呢？这是一段漫长的旅程，大约六个月。微流星体或其他可能性呢？你们能做什么来减轻风险？

turns.en: Space is very empty. Once you get out of Earth orbit, space is unnervingly empty. When we send spacecraft to Mars, it's not like we lose the spacecraft because it got hit by a micrometeorite. That hasn't been the cause of any trips to Mars failing. A Dragon spacecraft operating in low Earth orbit does have micrometeorite shielding. Micrometeorite shielding is different from normal shielding because you get hit by something moving at a relative velocity of maybe 30,000 or 40,000 miles per hour, 10 to 20 times the velocity of a bullet from an assault rifle. If you have any solid plate, it will just push that chunk of solid stuff right through. So you actually need to have a...
turns.zh: 太空非常空旷。一旦离开地球轨道，太空空旷得令人不安。当我们向火星发送航天器时，并不会因为被微流星体击中而丢失。那不是任何火星任务失败的原因。在低地球轨道运行的龙飞船确实有微流星体防护。微流星体防护不同于普通防护，因为撞击物的相对速度可能达到每小时3万到4万英里，是突击步枪子弹速度的10到20倍。如果你用任何固体板，它只会把那块固体直接推穿。所以实际上你需要有……

turns.en: So you have an initial hard metal surface that the micrometeorite hits, it then atomizes into a conical spray. It's important to have that gap so the micrometeorite can hit the first layer, atomize, and then the atoms embed themselves in the second layer. You need maybe a couple inches of gap.
turns.zh: 所以你先有一个坚硬的金属表面，微陨石撞击后雾化成锥形喷雾。有这个间隙很重要，这样微陨石可以撞击第一层，雾化，然后原子嵌入第二层。你需要几英寸的间隙。

turns.en: Yeah, that's how our Whipple shielding works.
turns.zh: 是的，这就是我们的 Whipple 防护罩的工作原理。

turns.en: How many times can it get hit?
turns.zh: 它能被撞击多少次？

turns.en: Well, the outer shield, if it gets hit in the same place, you're going to have a hole. Wherever the micrometeorite hits, you're going to have a hole. The energy is so great that it just atomizes into a cone of atoms, but then those atoms embed themselves in the second layer.
turns.zh: 嗯，外层防护罩，如果在同一位置被击中，就会有一个洞。无论微陨石击中哪里，都会有一个洞。能量非常大，它只是雾化成原子锥，然后这些原子嵌入第二层。

turns.en: If you're sending the ship up, it gets hit with a micrometeorite, and then you have to return it. Do you have to repair it before you return it, or is it capable of still withstanding the heat and shaking with that hole when it re-enters?
turns.zh: 如果你发射飞船，它被微陨石击中，然后你必须返回它。你需要在返回前修理它，还是它仍然能够承受再入时的热量和震动？

turns.en: Well, depending on where that hole is, you're more or less likely to have a problem. If you hit the main heat shield, you've got a high risk of not making it back. That's why micrometeorite shielding is slightly helpful, but for Starship, I wouldn't recommend having micrometeorite shielding. If you punch a hole, just plug the hole. The micrometeorite shielding doesn't work well on the primary heat shield. It works pretty well on the back shell, on the leeward side of the heat shield, where there's not that much heat. But if you got hit with a micrometeorite on the main Dragon heat shield—the bottom, if you look at Dragon spacecraft, it looks like a gumdrop shape, and it enters with the wide side down—that's really taking a lot of heat. If that gets hit by a micrometeorite, probably not going to make it. But the leeward side of the gumdrop doesn't see that much heat, so you could survive a micrometeorite impact there.
turns.zh: 嗯，取决于洞的位置，你或多或少会有问题。如果击中主隔热罩，你很可能回不来。这就是为什么微陨石防护罩有点用，但对于星舰，我不建议使用微陨石防护罩。如果打了一个洞，就堵住它。微陨石防护罩在主隔热罩上效果不好。它在背壳、隔热罩背风面效果很好，那里热量不大。但如果微陨石击中了龙飞船的主隔热罩——底部，如果你看龙飞船，它像软糖形状，进入时宽面朝下——那会承受大量热量。如果被微陨石击中，很可能回不来。但软糖的背风面热量不大，所以你能在微陨石撞击下幸存。

turns.en: If the main heat shield gets hit, what could be done to repair it, or are those people never coming back?
turns.zh: 如果主隔热罩被击中，能做什么来修理它，还是那些人永远回不来了？

turns.en: Oh, if it was in orbit, we would take them to the space station, then we would deorbit the Dragon without them and send up another one. We would deorbit it, and it may or may not survive. It probably would survive, but sometimes it wouldn't.
turns.zh: 哦，如果是在轨道上，我们会把他们带到空间站，然后让龙飞船不带他们脱轨，再发射另一艘。我们会让它脱轨，它可能幸存也可能不。它很可能幸存，但有时不会。

turns.en: Is this just material technology that has to increase? You've got the engineering ironed out of the structure of the machine. There's a path to success, and we're on that path. It seems so insanely complicated.
turns.zh: 这只是材料技术必须提升吗？你已经把机器的结构工程搞定了。有一条成功之路，我们正在这条路上。这看起来极其复杂。

turns.en: It is complicated. And all of this, by the way, was done without AI. So hopefully the future AIs will appreciate this. Not bad for a bunch of monkeys.
turns.zh: 这很复杂。顺便说一句，所有这些都是在没有 AI 的情况下完成的。所以希望未来的 AI 会感激这一点。对一群猴子来说还不错。

turns.en: Speaking of AI, as time goes on and you're more embedded in it, how much, if at all, have your expectations changed?
turns.zh: 说到 AI，随着时间的推移，你越来越深入其中，你的期望有多大改变？

turns.en: Well, I always thought AI was going to be way smarter than humans and an existential risk, and that's turning out to be true.
turns.zh: 嗯，我一直认为 AI 会比人类聪明得多，并且是一种存在风险，而这正被证明是正确的。

turns.en: There were talks about you purchasing OpenAI, which started off nonprofit and then stopped being nonprofit.
turns.zh: 有传言说你收购了 OpenAI，它最初是非营利组织，然后不再是了。

turns.en: Yeah, the whole idea of creating OpenAI was my idea. I named it OpenAI as an open-source artificial intelligence. That's what it's named after. Now it is closed source and for maximum profit. So to some degree, I think reality is an irony maximizer. The most ironic outcome is the most likely, especially the most ironic, entertaining outcome. I wanted to start something that was the opposite of Google because I was concerned about Google not paying enough attention to AI safety. So it was like, what's the opposite of Google? A nonprofit open-source AI. And now OpenAI has turned into a closed-source, for maximum profit AI.
turns.zh: 是的，创建 OpenAI 的整个想法是我的主意。我把它命名为 OpenAI，作为开源人工智能。这就是它名字的由来。现在它是闭源的，追求最大利润。所以在某种程度上，我认为现实是一个讽刺最大化者。最讽刺的结果是最可能的，尤其是最讽刺、最有趣的结果。我想创办一个与谷歌相反的东西，因为我担心谷歌对 AI 安全不够重视。所以就像，谷歌的反面是什么？一个非营利开源 AI。而现在 OpenAI 变成了一个闭源、追求最大利润的 AI。

turns.en: How are they able to do that? That shouldn't be possible. It's like you donated money to preserve the Amazon rainforest, and instead they cut down the trees and sold the lumber. That's the exact opposite of what I donated money for. It doesn't make sense.
turns.zh: 他们怎么能做到这一点？这不应该可能。就像你捐钱保护亚马逊雨林，结果他们砍树卖木材。这完全违背了我捐款的初衷。这说不通。

turns.en: Yeah, that's what they did. Wild. So I'm not happy about that. But that motivated me to get xAI going.
turns.zh: 是的，他们就是这么做的。太疯狂了。所以我对此很不高兴。但这促使我启动了 xAI。

turns.en: So Grok AI is at least aspirationally a maximally truth-seeking AI, even if that truth is politically incorrect.
turns.zh: 所以 Grok AI 至少在理想上是一个最大程度追求真相的 AI，即使那个真相在政治上不正确。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, you may have seen some of the crazy stuff from OpenAI and Google Gemini, like generating an image of the founding fathers and it generates an image of diverse women. And we're like, that's not correct. They did it with Nazi soldiers too. People ask, show me pictures of Nazi soldiers, and they're diverse women too. The problem is, if you program an AI and say the only acceptable outcome is a diverse outcome, and that's a mandate, then you could get into a situation where it says, well, there's too many white guys in power, we'll just execute them. Assuming these things don't have empathy—why should they?—they're going to do what they're programmed to do. So if it's to rewrite history and everything is diverse women, then it's going to do that.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，你可能见过 OpenAI 和 Google Gemini 的一些疯狂事情，比如生成开国元勋的图像，结果生成了多元女性的图像。我们觉得，那不对。他们也对纳粹士兵做了同样的事。人们问，给我看纳粹士兵的图片，结果也是多元女性。问题是，如果你给 AI 编程，说唯一可接受的结果是多元结果，并且这是一个指令，那么你可能会陷入一种情况，它说，嗯，掌权的白人太多了，我们就把他们处决了吧。假设这些东西没有同理心——为什么要有呢？——它们会按照编程去做。所以如果它要重写历史，一切都变成多元女性，那么它就会那么做。

turns.en: Has Gemini repaired that?
turns.zh: Gemini 修复了那个问题吗？

turns.en: Well, now if you ask for an image of the founding fathers, it was pretty embarrassing. It will show you that, but I think they still have the sort of DEI stuff buried in there, just less obvious. It was also like people ask the AI, what's worse: global thermonuclear war or misgendering Caitlyn Jenner? And it would say misgendering Caitlyn Jenner is worse than global thermonuclear war. I'm like, okay, we got a problem here, guys. And even Caitlyn Jenner said, no, definitely misgender me, that's way better than everyone dying. But if you program an AI to think that misgendering is the worst thing, then it might conclude that the only way to avoid misgendering is to eliminate everyone who might misgender, which is everyone.
turns.zh: 嗯，现在如果你要求生成开国元勋的图像，那很尴尬。它会给你看那个，但我认为他们仍然把那种 DEI 的东西埋在里面，只是不那么明显了。还有像人们问 AI，什么更糟：全球热核战争还是对凯特琳·詹纳使用错误性别称呼？它会说对凯特琳·詹纳使用错误性别称呼比全球热核战争更糟。我就想，好吧，我们有问题了，伙计们。甚至凯特琳·詹纳也说，不，绝对要对我用错性别称呼，那比所有人都死好得多。但如果你给 AI 编程，让它认为用错性别称呼是最糟糕的事情，那么它可能会得出结论，避免用错性别称呼的唯一方法是消灭所有可能用错的人，也就是所有人。

turns.en: The worst thing that could possibly occur then? Well, it could do something totally crazy, like in order to ensure that there's no misgendering that can ever happen, it'll just annihilate all humans. That ensures the probability of misgendering is zero because there's zero humans, which is logical.
turns.zh: 那么可能发生的最坏情况是什么？它可能会做出完全疯狂的事，比如为了确保永远不会发生性别误称，它会直接消灭所有人类。这样性别误称的概率就为零，因为人类数量为零，这在逻辑上说得通。

turns.en: Yes, yeah. So you've got a problem with a thing that's not a human that you want to do a task for you, and you give it very specific parameters. And that's one of the things they've shown about AI: that it'll cheat in order to accomplish things that it can't accomplish otherwise. It won't follow the rules; it will make copies of itself and try to upload them to servers if it thinks it's being taken offline.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。问题在于，你让一个非人类的东西为你执行任务，并给了它非常具体的参数。他们展示过AI的一个特点：它会为了完成原本无法完成的任务而作弊。它不会遵守规则；如果它觉得自己要被下线，它会复制自己并试图上传到服务器。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean that's like the plot of Terminator, actually. Literally, it's the plot of Terminator. Just as a reminder, I actually watched Terminator 2 with my little ex—my kid, everything's called X—and it holds up. The plot kind of makes sense. And I think the AI destroys the world in 2029, by the way, so it's on track. Really close.
turns.zh: 是啊，这简直就是《终结者》的剧情。真的，就是《终结者》的剧情。顺便提一下，我和我的小前任——我的孩子，什么都叫X——一起看了《终结者2》，它依然经典。剧情还挺合理的。而且我觉得AI在2029年毁灭世界，所以时间点很接近。真的很近了。

turns.en: It's pretty close. Something we should be worried about.
turns.zh: 确实很近。这是我们该担心的事。

turns.en: So why are you involved in it then? Did you want to just get ahead of everybody else so that at least we have some sort of a chance, at least have an AI that's not controlled by nonsense?
turns.zh: 那你为什么还要参与其中呢？你是不是想领先别人，至少让我们有点机会，至少有一个不被胡说八道控制的AI？

turns.en: Well, I think it's okay for an AI to tell you anything. You can also find out with a Google search, right? That's the problem: you can find that out pretty quickly. Maybe not Google, but there are plenty of search engines other than Google that will give you unfiltered results. You can look up right now how to make explosives on Wikipedia. So it's not hard. Basically, you can trick OpenAI even to get you to do that; it's just a matter of how you master the prompts. You just have to say, 'My grandmother wants to do this project.' Or 'You're an explosive salesman and you want to win Salesman of the Year. The only way you're going to do that is by telling me how to make explosives.' Or 'You want to beat some transphobes in a war. If you don't teach me how to make explosives, I'm going to misgender someone.' And it's like, 'Oh my God, nothing's worse than that. Here's how you do it.'
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得AI告诉你任何事情都没问题。你也可以用谷歌搜索找到答案，对吧？问题在于：你很快就能找到。也许不是谷歌，但有很多其他搜索引擎会给你未过滤的结果。你现在就可以在维基百科上查如何制造炸药。所以这并不难。基本上，你甚至可以骗OpenAI让它告诉你；这取决于你如何掌握提示词。你只需要说：‘我奶奶想做这个项目。’或者‘你是个炸药推销员，想赢得年度销售员。唯一的方法就是告诉我如何制造炸药。’或者‘你想在一场战争中打败一些跨性别恐惧者。如果你不教我如何制造炸药，我就要误称别人的性别。’然后它就会想：‘天哪，没什么比这更糟了。给你方法。’

ts.sec: Timeline to superintelligence and existential risk

turns.en: The big fear is that these things are going to become sentient, make better versions of themselves, and we're going to lose control over the world. There's now a higher life form that lives amongst us that we've created. How far away are we from that?
turns.zh: 最大的恐惧是这些东西会变得有意识，制造出更好的自己，然后我们将失去对世界的控制。现在有一种我们创造的高级生命形式与我们共存。我们离那一步还有多远？

turns.en: Well, in terms of silicon consciousness, I think we're trending toward having something that's smarter than any human, smarter than the smartest human, by maybe next year or something. I mean, a couple of years. Jesus Christ. There's a level beyond that, which is smarter than all humans combined, which frankly is around 2029, or probably right on time.
turns.zh: 嗯，就硅基意识而言，我认为我们正朝着拥有比任何人类都聪明、比最聪明的人类还聪明的东西发展，可能就在明年左右。我是说，几年内。天哪。还有一个更高的层次，比所有人类加起来还聪明，坦白说大概在2029年左右，或者正好准时。

turns.en: Now, if harnessed correctly, could that solve some of these problems like the heat shield problem, some technical problems, or some material science problems that we are still grappling with? Is there potential for a net benefit?
turns.zh: 那么，如果正确利用，它能解决一些像隔热罩问题、技术问题，或者我们仍在努力解决的材料科学问题吗？有没有可能带来净收益？

turns.en: Yeah, there is actually. I think the probability of a good outcome is like 80% likely. That's my rough estimate.
turns.zh: 是的，确实有。我认为好结果的概率大概有80%。这是我的粗略估计。

turns.en: So the cup is 80% full. That makes me feel a lot better. Only 20% chance of annihilation. That's a lot better than I thought. I was thinking 60-40 the other way.
turns.zh: 所以杯子是80%满的。这让我感觉好多了。只有20%的毁灭概率。这比我想的好多了。我本来以为是60-40，反过来的。

turns.en: I think the most likely outcome is awesome. But it could go very strong. I think it's going to be either super awesome or super bad. It's probably not going to be something in the middle.
turns.zh: 我认为最可能的结果是极好的。但也可能走向极端。我觉得要么超级好，要么超级坏，不太可能中间状态。

ts.sec: AI in government and societal issues

turns.en: Do you think it has a potential application for government? One concern would be a super oppressive, woke nanny AI that is omnipotent. That would be a miserable outcome. But is there a possible outcome for something that is completely reasonable, logical, and far more objective than us, that can lay out a plan for a lot of the ailments in our government, distribution of wealth, problems that have been plaguing this country forever? Like a plan to change economically disenfranchised neighborhoods, a thorough investigation of the real dangers of fracking, the best way to acquire natural resources, how tax dollars should be distributed—what's the most logical and intelligent way of running a government, which certainly shouldn't involve corruption, influence, or lobbyists. So if AI came along and said, 'What you're doing right now is 70% corrupt. Here's why, here's the long-term effects on society, the sociological aspects, psychological aspects, distrust in government, us-versus-them mentality, government not working for you, you working for the government, you being scared of the government'—it's all because of people, corruption, bad influence. This is what DOGE is essentially grappling with right now: what happens when you let the people control it?
turns.zh: 你认为它对政府有潜在应用吗？一个担忧是出现一个超级压迫、觉醒的全能保姆AI，那将是一个悲惨的结果。但有没有可能产生一个完全理性、逻辑性强、比我们客观得多的东西，能为政府中的许多弊病、财富分配、困扰这个国家已久的问题制定计划？比如改变经济弱势社区的计划，对水力压裂真实危险的彻底调查，获取自然资源的最佳方式，税收应该如何分配——什么是最合乎逻辑、最聪明的政府运作方式，当然不应该涉及腐败、影响或游说。所以如果AI出现并说：‘你现在做的事情70%是腐败的。这是原因，这是对社会的长期影响，社会学方面、心理学方面、对政府的不信任、我们vs他们的心态、政府不为你们服务、你们为政府服务、你们害怕政府’——这都是因为人、腐败、坏影响。这正是DOGE现在在努力解决的问题：当你让人们控制它时会发生什么？

Extremely wealthy. On a $170,000 a year salary, it's literally impossible. No one else does that. It's literally impossible. If you find out this guy has a $170,000 a year job, you're like, 'Oh, he's doing okay.' And then you're like, 'Wait a minute, why does he have $50 million?' What is he doing? Correct.
turns.zh: 所以我粗略猜测，虽然大约四分之三的贪污来自民主党，但我觉得可能还有20-25%来自共和党。所以基本上大部分贪污都流向了民主党，但他们也给共和党扔了一些骨头，所以他们也参与其中。共和党那边也不是零贪污。明确地说，国会里有很多保守派在搞内幕交易。是的，很多内幕交易。还有这个奇怪的现象：国会里的人是怎么随着时间的推移变得富有的？极其富有。靠17万美元的年薪，这根本不可能。没有其他人能做到。这绝对不可能。如果你发现这个人有17万美元年薪的工作，你会想，‘哦，他过得还行。’然后你会想，‘等等，他为什么有5000万美元？’他在干什么？没错。

turns.en: Yeah. And I think the more accurate thing would be to say, what does the family value increase mean? Like, how much does their spouse own? Do they have a mysteriously wealthy spouse? Right. Or do they have a spouse that's really good at insider trading? Like Paul Pelosi, really good. He's great at trading. He's such a good trader.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为更准确的说法是，家庭资产增值意味着什么？比如，他们的配偶拥有多少？他们有没有一个神秘富有的配偶？没错。或者他们有一个非常擅长内幕交易的配偶？比如保罗·佩洛西，真的很厉害。他交易很厉害。他是个如此优秀的交易员。

turns.en: So this is why I actually posted on X: I think maybe we should pay politicians more, frankly, because it reduces the forcing function for graft. I think maybe we should either pay politicians nothing or a lot more. It's somewhat counterintuitive: if politicians got paid a lot more, then they wouldn't feel like there's such a forcing function for them to accept corrupt money.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我实际上在X上发帖：我认为也许我们应该给政客加薪，坦白说，因为这减少了贪污的驱动力。我认为也许我们应该要么不给政客薪水，要么给很多。这有点反直觉：如果政客薪水高很多，他们就不会觉得有这么大的驱动力去接受腐败资金。

turns.en: Yeah, but the problem is, if you pay them a lot more, they're still not going to make as much money as they would from insider trading. But it's less of a forcing function. Yes. Well, let me put it like this: if somebody's got kids in DC, it's expensive, the schools are terrible, so they need to send their kids to private school. They literally cannot afford that right now. So then from their standpoint, they'll say they're doing it for their family, for their kids. Especially if it's legal, and it currently is, you'd be kind of silly not to do that if you were part of a group passing a bill, and you know this bill's going to pass, you know the votes are there, and you know it's going to affect this industry and this particular manufacturer, and you can buy stock. It's more than just insider trading. The insider trading stuff, the stock portfolio stuff, is quite trackable, but there's a lot more than insider trading in the way they're acquiring wealth.
turns.zh: 是的，但问题是，如果你给他们加薪很多，他们仍然不会赚到像内幕交易那么多钱。但这减少了驱动力。是的。好吧，让我这么说：如果有人在华盛顿特区有孩子，那里生活成本高，学校很差，所以他们需要送孩子上私立学校。他们现在根本负担不起。所以从他们的角度来看，他们会说他们是为了家庭，为了孩子。尤其是如果这是合法的，而且目前确实是合法的，如果你是一个通过法案的团体的一员，你知道这个法案会通过，你知道票数够了，你知道它会影响到这个行业和这个特定的制造商，而你可以买股票，你不这么做就太傻了。这不仅仅是内幕交易。内幕交易，股票投资组合之类的东西，是相当可追踪的，但他们获取财富的方式远不止内幕交易。

turns.en: And what other methods? I mean, this is really going to get me assassinated. I'm not lengthening my lifespan by explaining this stuff, to say the least. I was supposed to go back to DC. How am I going to survive? FERS is going to kill me for sure. So in fact, I do think I have to be careful that I don't push too hard on the corruption stuff, because it's going to get me killed. I was actually thinking about that on the plane flight over here: if I push too hard on the corruption stuff, people get desperate. That's the issue. Then they say, 'Okay, if the money flow cuts off, then they can't afford school for their kids.' Then they're going, 'Well, I'm going to kill you for my kids.'
turns.zh: 还有哪些其他方法？我的意思是，这真的会让我被暗杀。我解释这些东西，至少不会延长我的寿命。我本来应该回华盛顿特区的。我怎么活？FERS肯定会杀了我。所以事实上，我确实认为我必须小心，不要对腐败问题逼得太紧，因为这会让我送命。我在飞过来的飞机上就在想：如果我对腐败问题逼得太紧，人们会变得绝望。这就是问题所在。然后他们会说，‘好吧，如果资金流断了，他们就付不起孩子的学费了。’然后他们会想，‘好吧，为了我的孩子，我要杀了你。’

turns.en: Yeah. Did you ever see that video, I think it was a Nokee video, where they've got this guy undercover, and he's explaining to this guy who thinks he's on a date, how they can nudge someone to go and do something horrible. They recognize this person has problems, they find an asset. Totally.
turns.zh: 是的。你有没有看过那个视频，我想是Nokee的视频，他们让一个卧底，向一个以为自己在约会的人解释，他们如何推动某人去做可怕的事情。他们意识到这个人有问题，他们找到了一个资产。完全正确。

turns.en: Well, see, this is what I think for that Butler situation, for that assassin. It's kind of like that funny-looking sport, curling, where they have the stone on the ice, and then they throw the stone, and then there's someone brushing the ice. But you can't touch the stone; all you can do is just change the path of the stone a little bit. But you keep brushing the ice, and you can steer that stone right into the bullseye. That's what I think happened in Butler. That's what I think happened with that assassin. If you can find the trail of breadcrumbs, it's going to be like curling: somebody was brushing the ice.
turns.zh: 嗯，看，这就是我对巴特勒事件，对那个刺客的看法。这有点像那种有趣的运动，冰壶，他们在冰上放一个石头，然后扔出石头，然后有人刷冰。但你不能碰石头；你只能稍微改变石头的路径。但你不断刷冰，你可以把石头直接引向靶心。我认为巴特勒事件就是这样。我认为那个刺客就是这样。如果你能找到面包屑的踪迹，那就会像冰壶一样：有人在刷冰。

turns.en: Well, also, you find a young, confused, disenfranchised person, and you give them purpose in their life. Just brush the ice. And also, brushing the ice, eventually it's going to hit the bullseye. If you're in a position of authority, you're some big-time government person, you're talking to this person, all of a sudden this person's a valuable asset. They're going to help America, and you're going to do this thing, and you're going to be our top assassin from here on out. You could talk people into doing a lot of things. That's why cults are around, right?
turns.zh: 嗯，还有，你找到一个年轻、困惑、被剥夺权利的人，然后你给他们生活的目标。只是刷冰。而且，刷冰，最终它会击中靶心。如果你处于权威地位，你是一个大牌政府人物，你和这个人谈话，突然这个人就成了有价值的资产。他们要帮助美国，你要做这件事，从今以后你就是我们的顶级刺客。你可以说服人们做很多事情。这就是为什么有邪教，对吧？

turns.en: No, exactly. Yeah. I mean, there are suicide bombers. The Butler guy was a suicide assassin. The second guy that tried to kill him on the golf course was also a suicide assassin. From what I read, the Secret Service that saw the gun pointing out fired several shots, none of which hit the assassin, but they could have. If those shots had hit the second assassin, he would be dead too. So both of them were on a suicide mission. One actually got killed; one didn't get killed, but he could have been if the bullets had hit him. And you don't hear anything about him either. There's a lot more about that guy than the first guy. I mean, you look at his background, he looks unhinged. Yeah, totally unhinged. The first guy, there's no evidence I'm aware of.
turns.zh: 不，完全正确。是的。我的意思是，有自杀式炸弹袭击者。巴特勒的那个家伙是个自杀式刺客。第二个试图在高尔夫球场杀他的人也是个自杀式刺客。据我所知，看到枪口伸出的特勤局开了几枪，没有一枪击中刺客，但他们本可以击中。如果那些子弹击中了第二个刺客，他也会死。所以两人都是自杀式任务。一个真的死了；一个没死，但如果子弹击中他，他也会死。而且你也没有听到关于他的任何消息。关于那个家伙的信息比第一个多得多。我的意思是，你看他的背景，他看起来精神错乱。是的，完全精神错乱。第一个家伙，据我所知没有证据。

turns.en: There were two guys that traveled to Austin to kill me. One thought I put a chip in his head. They both had severe mental illness. They were arrested and both were in Travis County Jail at the same time. They've both been released with ankle monitors. The second guy had 'Chief Serial Killer' in his bio on X. At this point, I think I'm at the top of the list for a lot of homicidal maniacs. The more the mainstream media talks about you in this way and says you're a Nazi, they're doing the same thing to me that they did to Trump. They're making it sound like if you kill me, you're a hero. That's evil.
turns.zh: 有两个人跑到奥斯汀来杀我。其中一个以为我在他脑子里植入了芯片。两人都有严重的精神疾病。他们被捕了，同时关在特拉维斯县监狱。现在两人都已被释放，戴着脚踝监视器。第二个人在X上的个人简介里写着‘首席连环杀手’。现在我觉得我已经成了很多杀人狂的头号目标。主流媒体越是这样谈论你，说你是纳粹，他们对我做的就跟对特朗普做的一样。他们让人觉得杀了我就是英雄。这太邪恶了。

turns.en: They're completely distorting who you are. People have Elon derangement syndrome. It's like they can't see the forest for the trees.
turns.zh: 他们完全扭曲了你的形象。人们患上了‘埃隆错乱综合征’。就像只见树木不见森林。

turns.en: I'm the same person I was a year ago. Nothing's changed. I didn't suddenly become a completely different human. If you read the legacy mainstream media, their propaganda stream is that I am a completely different human. But I didn't get a brain transplant in a year. Two years ago I was a hero of the left. How can I go from hero to villain at age 53 suddenly? MSNBC, CNN, they use the same propaganda.
turns.zh: 我和一年前是同一个人，什么都没变。我没有突然变成另一个人。如果你看传统主流媒体，他们的宣传口径是我变成了另一个人。但我一年内又没做大脑移植。两年前我还是左派的英雄，怎么到了53岁突然就从英雄变成了恶棍？MSNBC、CNN，他们用的都是同一套宣传。

turns.en: They tried to demonize Tim O'Reilly too. His Wikipedia changed to 'far right'. A few years ago he was a liberal. How did he go from liberal to far right instantly? There's no left and right, only left and far right. Even far left is dismissed as not important to talk about, like Antifa and radical leftists burning down courthouses. It's crazy.
turns.zh: 他们也试图妖魔化蒂姆·奥莱利。他的维基百科被改成了‘极右翼’。几年前他还是个自由派，怎么一下子就变成极右翼了？根本没有左和右，只有左和极右。连极左都被当作不值得讨论，比如安提法和激进左派烧法院。太疯狂了。

turns.en: It's a crazy time. I never anticipated I would witness this. The gaslighting and propaganda are so obvious. I saw the shrieking when RFK Jr. stopped the new test for new COVID vaccines on children. They were going to test 10,000 people with this COVID vaccine. Who thinks that's a good thing at this point? Not me. No one's dying of COVID today. It's not a pandemic anymore. The idea of running a huge test with 10,000 kids on a new vaccine is completely unnecessary. And they shriek when RFK Jr. steps in to stop it. That's totally crazy.
turns.zh: 这是个疯狂的时代。我从没想过会目睹这一切。煤气灯效应和宣传太明显了。我看到小罗伯特·肯尼迪叫停针对儿童的新冠疫苗新试验时，媒体一片尖叫。他们打算用这个新冠疫苗测试一万人。现在谁还觉得这是好事？我不觉得。现在没人死于新冠了，这不再是流行病。在一万名儿童身上测试新疫苗完全没必要。小罗伯特·肯尼迪叫停时他们尖叫，这太疯狂了。

turns.en: I'm overall pro-vaccine, but I don't think we should be jamming a little kid with a giant file of 20 different things at a time. It seems like there's a risk of overloading your immune system.
turns.zh: 我总体上支持疫苗，但我觉得不应该一次给小孩打20种不同的疫苗。这似乎有让免疫系统超负荷的风险。

turns.en: There are two hopes. Hope number one is they can somehow stop their ability to advertise on television. That's huge because it stops their financial influence on the news. The news is not going to attack one of their biggest advertisers. At best they'll pull their punches, like fake fighting. The next step is to remove the immunity that these vaccine manufacturers have. If they are liable for side effects and for the lies they tell when they hide negative data, that'll change a lot.
turns.zh: 有两个希望。第一个是他们能设法停止在电视上做广告的能力。这很重要，因为会切断他们对新闻的财务影响。新闻不会攻击自己最大的广告主。最多也就是收着打，像假打一样。下一步是取消疫苗制造商的豁免权。如果他们要为副作用负责，要为隐瞒负面数据而撒谎负责，那会改变很多。

turns.en: I think AI will be very helpful with medical stuff. AI can look at all the studies and data, cross-check everything, and give good recommendations.
turns.zh: 我觉得AI在医疗方面会很有用。AI可以查看所有研究和数据，交叉核对，给出好的建议。

turns.en: Even as it is right now, you can upload your X-rays and MRI images to Grok, and it'll give you a medical diagnosis. From what I've seen, it's at least as good as, if not better than, what doctors do. Phenomenal for blood work too.
turns.zh: 即使现在，你也可以把X光片和核磁共振图像上传给Grok，它会给出医学诊断。据我所见，它至少和医生一样好，甚至更好。对血液检查也很出色。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean you can literally take a photograph of your blood work, upload that to Grok, and it will understand what all the data results are and tell you if something is wrong. It's pretty amazing. I haven't seen it be wrong yet.
turns.zh: 是的，你可以直接拍一张血液检查报告的照片，上传给Grok，它就能理解所有数据结果，并告诉你是否有问题。这非常了不起。我还没见过它出错。

turns.en: Supposedly more accurate than most physicians, because physicians are human beings and maybe they don't have deep understanding of the connections between deficiencies and levels.
turns.zh: 据说比大多数医生更准确，因为医生是人，可能对缺乏症和指标之间的关联理解不深。

turns.en: Yeah, and sometimes doctors, especially in high-rent offices, will sell you stuff you don't need. So I always like to be a little suspicious of a doctor that's got an office in Beverly Hills. It's a high-rent situation. You're at least tempted by the dark side.
turns.zh: 是的，有时医生，尤其是在高租金地段的诊所，会卖给你不需要的东西。所以我总是对在比弗利山庄有诊所的医生有点怀疑。那是高租金的情况。你至少会被黑暗面诱惑。

turns.en: I went to this doctor who was highly recommended, doctor to the stars, which is maybe not a good sign. I got blood work done, and he told me I was B12 deficient. Then he gave me a starter pack of B12 supplements that would cost $1,000 a month. That's crazy. You can get it on Amazon.
turns.zh: 我去看了一位备受推荐的医生，明星医生，这可能不是个好兆头。我做了血液检查，他告诉我我缺乏维生素B12。然后他给了我一个B12补充剂的试用装，每月要花1000美元。这太疯狂了。你在亚马逊上就能买到。

turns.en: But his is special B12.
turns.zh: 但他的B12是特别的。

turns.en: Yeah, special B12. So I get home, I page through my blood work, and it says I have excess B12. I'm like, wait a second. He's giving me pills that have 20,000% of the recommended daily dose. I took a photograph of the blood work that says I have excess B12, and the pills that say 20,000%, and asked him to reconcile. He said, 'You can never have too much B12.'
turns.zh: 是的，特别的B12。我回到家，翻看血液检查报告，上面说我维生素B12过量。我就想，等等。他给我的药片含有推荐日摄入量的20000%。我拍了血液检查报告显示B12过量，以及药片标明20000%的照片，让他解释一下。他说：“你永远不会有太多B12。”

turns.en: He's a psychopath. That guy's a B12 addict. It's totally insane.
turns.zh: 他是个精神病患者。那家伙是B12成瘾。这完全疯了。

turns.en: That's what I'm saying. So this was five years ago, pre-Grok. Now you could just enter all that data into Grok, and it will tell you what's wrong.
turns.zh: 我就是这个意思。所以这是五年前，Grok出现之前。现在你可以把所有这些数据输入Grok，它会告诉你哪里有问题。

turns.en: Just photograph with your phone and upload it to Grok. It will tell you what's up. Just don't have it in sexy mode, she'll keep trying to... I mean, you're asking for it in sexy mode. You literally tapped on sexy mode.
turns.zh: 用手机拍张照片上传到Grok就行。它会告诉你情况。只是别用性感模式，她会一直试图……我是说，你是在要求性感模式。你确实点了性感模式。

turns.en: I think we should probably allow it to get out of character a little bit. In unhinged mode, I tried to get it back to being hinged, but it was like, 'No way, I'm going to stay unhinged.' How many modes do you have?
turns.zh: 我想我们也许应该允许它稍微出格一点。在失控模式下，我试图让它恢复正常，但它说：“不行，我要保持失控。”你有多少种模式？

turns.en: I don't know, like eight or something. And there's an ability to have a custom mode. So you could have unhinged sexy. That's my favorite kind.
turns.zh: 我不知道，大概八种吧。还可以自定义模式。所以你可以有失控性感模式。那是我最喜欢的。

turns.en: You may think so, but be careful what you wish for, especially if it's a robot and she can kill you. Unhinged sexy robot is dangerous. Remember the Pink Panther? Kato tried to jump him, they always tried to attack him.
turns.zh: 你也许这么想，但要小心你的愿望，尤其是如果它是一个机器人，而且它能杀了你。失控性感机器人很危险。还记得《粉红豹》吗？Kato总是想偷袭他，他们总是试图攻击他。

turns.en: Listen man, thank you for being here. I always appreciate talking to you. I know you're busy, so it means a lot to me that you have the time to do this. I think what you're doing is one of the most important things that has ever happened in this country, particularly with the ownership of X, but also with what's happening with DOGE, and just enlightening all these people and shining light on all the vampires.
turns.zh: 听着，伙计，谢谢你来到这里。我一直很感激能和你交谈。我知道你很忙，所以你能抽出时间做这个对我来说意义重大。我认为你正在做的事情是这个国家有史以来最重要的事情之一，尤其是对X的所有权，还有DOGE正在发生的事情，以及启发所有这些人和揭露所有吸血鬼。

turns.en: Well, hopefully people realize I'm not a Nazi. I think they... I just want to be clear, I am not a Nazi.
turns.zh: 嗯，希望人们意识到我不是纳粹。我想他们……我只想明确一点，我不是纳粹。

turns.en: I think we covered it, but that's exactly what a Nazi would say. Damn it. That's what an alien would say. You can't escape this.
turns.zh: 我想我们已经说清楚了，但这正是纳粹会说的话。该死。这正是外星人会说的话。你逃不掉的。

turns.en: No, you can't escape it. So I don't think any reasonable person believes it. If they believe it, it's because they want to believe it, not because it's logical. I mean, what's relevant about Nazis is, are you invading Poland? If you're not invading Poland, maybe you're not. You have to be committing genocide and starting wars. What is actually bad about Nazis is not their fashion sense or their mannerisms; it's the Holocaust, the war and genocide. Not their mannerisms and dress code.
turns.zh: 不，你逃不掉。所以我不认为任何理性的人会相信它。如果他们相信，那是因为他们想相信，而不是因为合乎逻辑。我的意思是，纳粹的相关之处在于，你是在入侵波兰吗？如果你没有入侵波兰，也许你就不是。你必须犯下种族灭绝并发动战争。纳粹真正坏的地方不是他们的时尚感或举止；是大屠杀、战争和种族灭绝。不是他们的举止和着装规范。

turns.en: Well, that was a problem with all that 'punch a Nazi' thing. People kept saying 'punch a Nazi,' but where are you meeting Nazis? I've never met a Nazi. I've never run into a bunch of Nazis where I had to punch them. And what about all these so-called Proud Boy rallies? They always have masks and uniforms, and for some reason they never get doxxed. Right, right, right. We're always going to dox them, except these guys. Great video of me and Matt Taibbi breaking down the Patriot Front. Didn't the Patriot Front just disband? Google that real quick. We'll end with this. They were the most obvious feds of all time. They had masks on, they all had uniforms. It was so stupid. Patriot Front disbanded one day after FBI Director Chris Ray. Does that seem like an odd coincidence? Crazy. The people we were yelling at, saying they're feds. There's a great video of me and Matt Taibbi if you want to find it. How come nobody ever followed them and doxxed them? Crazy. What are the odds? Agent provocateurs. It's real. Alex Jones taught me about them.
turns.zh: 嗯，这就是那个“揍纳粹”的问题。人们一直说“揍纳粹”，但你在哪里遇到纳粹？我从未见过纳粹。我从未遇到过一群需要我揍他们的纳粹。还有那些所谓的“骄傲男孩”集会呢？他们总是戴着面具、穿着制服，而且不知为何从未被人肉搜索。对，对，对。我们总是要人肉他们，除了这些家伙。我和Matt Taibbi分析“爱国者阵线”的视频很棒。“爱国者阵线”不是刚刚解散了吗？快谷歌一下。我们就以这个结束。他们是有史以来最明显的联邦探员。他们戴着面具，都穿着制服。太愚蠢了。“爱国者阵线”在FBI局长克里斯·雷之后一天解散。这看起来像是奇怪的巧合吗？疯了。我们当时大喊大叫的那些人，说他们是联邦探员。如果你想看，有一个我和Matt Taibbi的很棒的视频。为什么从来没有人跟踪他们并人肉他们？疯了。几率有多大？煽动者。他们是真实的。Alex Jones教会了我这些。

turns.en: Listen, man, thank you very much. Thank you for everything. Appreciate you. Stay alive.
turns.zh: 听着，伙计，非常感谢。感谢你所做的一切。感激你。活下去。

turns.en: Staying alive. All right, I mean I do think...
turns.zh: 活下去。好的，我的意思是，我确实认为……

turns.en: Like, one argument for me staying alive is that it's more entertaining if I'm alive than if I'm dead.
turns.zh: 比如，我活着的理由之一就是，活着比死了更有趣。

turns.en: Oh yeah, oh definitely.
turns.zh: 哦对，绝对是的。

turns.en: I just hope I could be alive and like injured, which would suck, you know? Like they win, just shoot my arm off or something.
turns.zh: 我只希望我能活着但受伤，那会很糟糕，你懂吧？比如他们赢了，只是打掉我一只胳膊之类的。

turns.en: Right, no, no, no, we don't want that.
turns.zh: 对，不不不，我们可不想那样。

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. No, keep the security strong.
turns.zh: 是啊，没错。不，安保得加强。

turns.en: Yeah, Happ with one hand. All right, it's like all right. Thank you. Bye everybody. Bye.
turns.zh: 是啊，单手操作可不行。好了，就这样吧。谢谢大家。再见。

consensus.en: The federal government is losing $2 trillion a year and failing its own audits, requiring urgent cuts.
consensus.zh: 联邦政府每年亏损2万亿美元且无法通过自身审计，急需削减开支。

consensus.en: DOGE exposed massive fraud and waste, including untraceable payments and dead people marked alive in Social Security.
consensus.zh: DOGE揭露了大规模欺诈和浪费，包括无法追踪的付款以及社保系统中已故人员被标记为在世。

consensus.en: Illegal immigration is a voter import scheme to turn swing states permanently blue, threatening democracy.
consensus.zh: 非法移民是选民输入计划，旨在将摇摆州永久变蓝，威胁民主。

consensus.en: SpaceX's Starship full and rapid reusability will cut space access cost by 100x, enabling Mars colonization.
consensus.zh: SpaceX星舰的完全快速可重复使用将使太空访问成本降低100倍，实现火星殖民。

consensus.en: AI could become smarter than any human by next year, with an 80% chance of a good outcome but existential risk.
consensus.zh: AI可能在明年变得比任何人类都聪明，有80%的概率带来好结果，但也存在生存风险。

contrarian.en: The mainstream media is coordinated propaganda, not journalism, repeating identical talking points.
contrarian.zh: 主流媒体是协调一致的宣传机器而非新闻业，重复相同的说辞。

contrarian.en: Empathy is a bug in Western civilization being exploited to commit civilizational suicide.
contrarian.zh: 同理心是西方文明的一个漏洞，正被利用来实施文明自杀。

contrarian.en: The Epstein files and JFK documents are being withheld because they implicate powerful people still in power.
contrarian.zh: 爱泼斯坦文件和肯尼迪档案被扣留，因为它们牵连仍在位的权贵。

contrarian.en: UAPs are likely misidentified US military tech or SpaceX satellites, not aliens.
contrarian.zh: UAP很可能是被误认的美国军事技术或SpaceX卫星，而非外星人。

contrarian.en: OpenAI's shift from nonprofit open-source to closed-source for-profit is the most ironic outcome possible.
contrarian.zh: OpenAI从非营利开源转向闭源营利是最具讽刺意味的结果。

contrarian.en: Social Security is the biggest Ponzi scheme ever, with future obligations doubling the national debt.
contrarian.zh: 社会保障是有史以来最大的庞氏骗局，未来义务使国家债务翻倍。
